technical_definition: A parallel merging algorithm that takes a bitonic sequence and produces a sorted sequence in O(log n) parallel steps.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing bitonic merge 'butterfly' comparisons]
common_misconceptions: It merges two sorted lists. (False: It specifically merges a 'bitonic' list, where one half is up and one half is down).

comparisons.note: Standard Merge Step is sequential; Bitonic Mergesort is fully parallel.

definition_short: Sorting data that is too large to fit into the computer's memory (RAM).
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a 100GB file but only 8GB of RAM. You can't open the whole file. Instead, you read 8GB chunks, sort them, and save them as small 'runs' on the Hard Drive. Then, you 'merge' those runs together to create the final sorted 100GB file.
real_world_example: A bank sorting a trillion transactions that are stored on massive hard drives.
technical_definition: A class of sorting algorithms that can handle massive amounts of data by utilizing secondary storage (disk) and minimizing expensive disk read/write operations.
syntax_or_example: // Phase 1: Sort chunks in RAM. Phase 2: Merge chunks from Disk.
common_misconceptions: You can use Quick Sort for this. (False: Quick Sort jumps around too much, which is very slow on a Hard Drive. External sorting almost always uses Merge Sort).

comparisons.note: Internal sort happens entirely in RAM; External sort uses the Hard Drive/Disk.

definition_short: Sorting processes that take place entirely within the computer's main memory (RAM).
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is the 'Normal' way of sorting. If your list of numbers is small enough to fit inside your computer's RAM, you can access any part of it instantly. This makes sorting very fast because the computer doesn't have to wait for a slow hard drive.
real_world_example: Sorting a list of 1,000 names on a modern laptop—the whole list fits easily in memory.
technical_definition: A class of sorting algorithms used when the entire dataset to be sorted can be held in the main memory at once.
syntax_or_example: // Standard sort() functions in C++, Java, or Python use internal sorting.
common_misconceptions: It is a specific algorithm. (False: Internal sorting is a category that includes Quick Sort, Merge Sort, and Bubble Sort).

comparisons.note: Internal is for RAM; External is for data that exceeds RAM capacity.

definition_short: Merging more than two sorted lists into one sorted result simultaneously.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Instead of just merging two lines of people, imagine merging 5 or 10 lines at the same time. You look at the front of every line, pick the absolute smallest person, and move them to the final line. It is much more efficient than merging two-by-two.
real_world_example: A website combining search results from 5 different databases into one single sorted list for the user.
technical_definition: An algorithm that merges K sorted input streams into a single sorted output stream, typically using a Min-Heap to find the minimum among the K elements in O(log K) time.
syntax_or_example: \n// Complexity: O(N log K) where N is total elements.
common_misconceptions: It is slower than 2-way merging. (False: For large numbers of lists, K-way merging reduces the number of times you have to 'read' the data from the disk).

comparisons.note: Standard Merge Step is 2-way; K-way handles many inputs at once.

definition_short: An algorithm used in external sorting to create longer sorted 'runs' initially.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are sorting a giant pile of papers. You have a small desk (RAM). You keep the smallest papers on your desk. As you find a new paper that is bigger than the last one you put away, you add it to your desk and send the smallest one out. This helps create sorted chunks that are much larger than your desk!
real_world_example: A factory belt where a machine picks the smallest part available to pack, while new parts keep arriving.
technical_definition: An external sorting technique that uses a heap to produce sorted sequences (runs) that are, on average, twice the size of the available memory.
common_misconceptions: It sorts the whole file. (False: It only creates the initial 'sorted pieces' which must then be merged together).

comparisons.note: Replacement Selection is the first phase; K-way Merging is the second phase.

definition_short: An efficient version of external merge sort that reduces the number of temporary files/tapes needed.
explanation: Simple Explanation: In the old days, data was stored on tapes. You couldn't just jump around. Polyphase merging is a clever way to shuffle data between tapes so that you never have to stop and 'rewind' or wait. It uses Fibonacci math to stay efficient.
real_world_example: A multi-stage recycling plant that moves items between bins so that no bin is ever completely empty or overflowing.
technical_definition: A variant of external merge sort that uses an uneven distribution of runs across available storage devices to minimize data copying.
syntax_or_example: // Based on Fibonacci sequences for run distribution.
common_misconceptions: It's used on modern SSDs. (False: This was specifically designed for sequential devices like magnetic tapes; modern systems usually just use standard K-way merges).

comparisons.note: Standard Merge Sort uses 2 files per level; Polyphase can use fewer.

definition_short: A sorting method that uses a tree to find the minimum element faster than standard Selection Sort.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Standard Selection Sort is slow because it scans the whole list to find the minimum. Tree Selection Sort builds a 'Tournament Tree' (like a sports bracket). Once the winner is found and removed, you only have to update one path of the tree to find the next winner. It's the ancestor of Heap Sort.
real_world_example: A talent show where winners move up a bracket, and if one drops out, you only re-judge their specific bracket to find a replacement.
technical_definition: A refinement of selection sort that uses a complete binary tree to find the next smallest element in O(log n) time.
common_misconceptions: It is as efficient as Heap Sort. (False: It uses more memory because it stores the whole tree structure explicitly, whereas Heap Sort uses a clever array trick).

comparisons.note: Heap Sort is the 'memory-efficient' version of Tree Selection Sort.

definition_short: Breadth-First Search; an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine throwing a stone into a pond. The ripples move outward in circles. BFS does the same—it visits all neighbors at distance 1, then all neighbors at distance 2, and so on. It's the best way to find the shortest path in an unweighted map.
real_world_example: A social network finding your 'friends' (Level 1), then 'friends of friends' (Level 2).
technical_definition: An algorithm that starts at a source node and explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth prior to moving on to the nodes at the next depth level.
syntax_or_example: \nqueue.push(root); while(!q.empty()) { ... }
common_misconceptions: It uses a stack. (False: BFS requires a Queue to maintain the correct layer-by-layer order).

comparisons.note: BFS goes 'wide' (layers); DFS goes 'deep' (branches).

definition_short: Depth-First Search; a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are in a maze. You pick a path and keep walking until you hit a wall. Then you go back (backtrack) to the last intersection and try a different path. It's great for checking if two things are connected or solving puzzles.
real_world_example: Solving a Sudoku or a maze by trial and error.
technical_definition: An algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures that starts at the root and explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
syntax_or_example: \nvoid dfs(v) { visited[v]=true; for(u : adj[v]) if(!visited[u]) dfs(u); }
common_misconceptions: It finds the shortest path. (False: It finds *any* path. The first path it finds might be very long and roundabout).

comparisons.note: DFS uses a Stack (or recursion); BFS uses a Queue.

definition_short: A specific type of tree traversal that follows the BFS strategy.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It means visiting every node in a tree level by level. You start at the root (Level 0), then do all the children (Level 1), then all the grandchildren (Level 2).
real_world_example: Reading a family tree generation by generation.
technical_definition: A traversal that visits all nodes at the current depth level before moving to nodes at the next depth level.
common_misconceptions: It's different from BFS. (False: In trees, 'Level Order' is simply the name we give to BFS).

comparisons.note: Level Order is horizontal; In-order is vertical/recursive.

definition_short: A tree traversal following the pattern: Root, Left, Right.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You visit the 'Parent' first, then you go into the left branch, then the right branch. It’s the way you would read a table of contents: Chapter title first, then the sub-sections.
real_world_example: Creating a copy of a tree structure—you must create the root node before you can attach children to it.
technical_definition: A depth-first traversal where each node is processed before its subtrees.
syntax_or_example: Process(node); Traverse(node->left); Traverse(node->right);
common_misconceptions: It's only for binary trees. (False: You can do pre-order on any tree; you visit the parent, then all children from left to right).

comparisons.note: Pre-order is 'Top-down'; Post-order is 'Bottom-up'.

definition_short: A tree traversal following the pattern: Left, Root, Right.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is special for Binary Search Trees (BSTs). If you do an In-order traversal of a BST, the numbers will come out in perfect sorted order (1, 2, 3...). It’s like reading a book from left to right.
real_world_example: Printing a list of all products in a database from cheapest to most expensive.
technical_definition: A depth-first traversal where the left subtree is visited first, then the node itself, and finally the right subtree.
syntax_or_example: Traverse(node->left); Process(node); Traverse(node->right);
common_misconceptions: It always results in sorted order. (False: This only happens if the tree is a Binary Search Tree).

comparisons.note: In-order puts the root in the middle; Pre-order puts the root at the start.

definition_short: A tree traversal following the pattern: Left, Right, Root.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You visit all the 'Children' first, and you visit the 'Parent' last. This is perfect for deleting things. You wouldn't delete a folder until you've deleted every file inside it first.
real_world_example: Calculating the total size of a folder—you must add up all the files inside before you know the folder's total.
technical_definition: A depth-first traversal where each node is processed after its subtrees have been processed.
syntax_or_example: Traverse(node->left); Traverse(node->right); Process(node);
common_misconceptions: It is slower than pre-order. (False: They all visit every node exactly once and take the same total time).

comparisons.note: Post-order handles children before parents; Pre-order handles parents before children.

definition_short: A strategy for searching or traversing that focuses on width before depth.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Wide' approach. If you are looking for a lost key in your house, Breadth First means checking every room on the ground floor completely before you go upstairs to the next floor.
real_world_example: A police search party spreading out to cover a whole field together, rather than one person walking a long zigzag path.
technical_definition: An exploration strategy that prioritizes nodes at the same distance from the start node.
common_misconceptions: It is always better than Depth First. (False: It uses much more memory because it has to remember every neighbor it sees on a level).

comparisons.note: Breadth First is better for 'Closest' targets; Depth First is better for 'Path' puzzles.

definition_short: A strategy for searching or traversing that focuses on going deep before going wide.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Deep' approach. If you are looking for a lost key, you go into one room, then into its closet, then into a box inside that closet. You only come back out to the main room if you find nothing in that deep path.
real_world_example: Exploring a cave system—you follow one tunnel until it ends before trying a different tunnel.
technical_definition: An exploration strategy that prioritizes following a single path to its conclusion before exploring other paths.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a deep vertical search path in a maze]
common_misconceptions: It's only for trees. (False: It is used for all kinds of graphs and complex problem-solving).

comparisons.note: Depth First uses less memory; Breadth First finds shortest paths.

definition_short: Cutting off branches of a search tree that are guaranteed to not have the answer.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Don't Waste Time' rule. Imagine looking for a red apple in a box of blue toys. Once you see the box is full of blue, you stop looking inside it and move to the next box. You 'pruned' (cut off) that whole box from your search.
real_world_example: A computer playing Chess: it ignores moves that would immediately lead to losing its Queen.
technical_definition: A technique in search algorithms that reduces the size of the search tree by removing branches that cannot contain a solution.
syntax_or_example: if (current_cost > best_cost_found) return; // Stop searching this branch.
common_misconceptions: Pruning is just a small speed boost. (False: In complex games like Chess or Go, pruning is the *only* reason computers can win; without it, there are too many possibilities to check).

comparisons.note: Backtracking is the search method; Pruning is the trick to make it faster.

definition_short: Finding a solution by exploring every possible 'state' or configuration of a problem.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a Rubik's Cube. Every time you turn it, you create a new 'state'. State space search is the process of trying different turns to see which sequence of states leads to the solved cube.
real_world_example: A GPS finding a route: every intersection is a 'state', and the search finds the best path between them.
technical_definition: A process used in AI where a problem is solved by defining states and transitions, and searching through the resulting graph of possibilities.
common_misconceptions: It only works for games. (False: It's used for everything from robot movement to scheduling airline flights).

comparisons.note: DFS is one specific algorithm; State Space Search is the general name for the problem type.

definition_short: Finding a solution that obeys a set of strict rules (constraints).
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s like a puzzle with rules. For example, 'Pack these 10 boxes into a car, but Box A cannot be near Box B, and the heavy ones must be on the bottom'. You aren't just searching; you are trying to find a configuration that doesn't break any rules.
real_world_example: Scheduling classes: Two teachers can't be in the same room at the same time.
technical_definition: A mathematical problem defined as a set of objects whose state must satisfy a number of constraints or limitations.
syntax_or_example: Variable: X, Value: {1,2,3}, Rule: X != 2.
common_misconceptions: There is always a solution. (False: Sometimes the rules are so strict that no possible answer exists).

comparisons.note: Constraint Satisfaction asks 'Can we do it?'; Optimization asks 'What's the best way to do it?'

definition_short: The challenge of placing N chess queens on an N×N board so that no two queens attack each other.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is a classic 'Logic Test' for computers. Queens can move in any direction (straight or diagonal). You have to place 8 queens so that no queen is in the same row, column, or diagonal as another. It’s the perfect example of 'Backtracking'.
real_world_example: A company assigning 8 different projects to 8 different managers so that no manager's schedules or resources overlap.
technical_definition: A backtracking problem that involves placing n non-attacking queens on an n×n chessboard.
common_misconceptions: It only works for 8. (False: You can solve it for any number N, like 4-queens or 100-queens).

comparisons.note: Both are constraint problems, but N-Queens focuses on diagonal and straight-line paths.

definition_short: An algorithm that fills a 9x9 grid with numbers 1-9 following Sudoku rules.
explanation: Simple Explanation: A computer solves Sudoku using 'Brute Force with Backtracking'. It puts a '1' in a blank spot. If that doesn't break a rule, it moves to the next spot. If it gets stuck, it goes back and changes that '1' to a '2'. It keeps doing this until the whole grid is full.
real_world_example: An automated system assigning hotel rooms to guests based on their specific requests (non-smoking, balcony, etc.).
technical_definition: A backtracking algorithm designed to solve Sudoku puzzles by recursively attempting to fill digits while satisfying row, column, and block constraints.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a Sudoku grid being solved with backtracking steps]
common_misconceptions: It uses 'logic' like humans. (False: While humans look for patterns, basic computer solvers just try every number until they find one that works).

comparisons.note: Sudoku has 9 possible moves per spot; Rat in a Maze usually has 2-4 moves.

definition_short: A sequence of moves by a chess knight visiting every square on a board exactly once.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Can a Knight visit all 64 squares on a chessboard without stepping on the same square twice? This is a very hard puzzle for computers because there are millions of possible paths. It uses backtracking to find the right sequence.
real_world_example: A delivery truck that must visit every house in a neighborhood exactly once and return home.
technical_definition: A mathematical problem in which the knight's goal is to visit every square on the board exactly once using its unique 'L-shaped' move.
common_misconceptions: There is only one path. (False: There are billions of different ways a knight can complete a tour on an 8x8 board).

comparisons.note: The Knight's Tour is a specific version of a Hamiltonian Path problem.

definition_short: A classic backtracking problem where a 'rat' must find a path from start to finish in a grid.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a grid where some squares are walls and some are open paths. The rat starts at Top-Left and wants to reach Bottom-Right. The computer tries going Down; if it hits a wall, it tries going Right. If it gets stuck, it 'backtracks' to the last turn.
real_world_example: A robot vacuum finding its way from one corner of a cluttered room to its charging station.
technical_definition: A standard pathfinding problem solved using recursion and backtracking by exploring possible directions (U, D, L, R) in a matrix.
common_misconceptions: It finds the shortest path. (False: Standard backtracking finds *any* valid path; to find the shortest, you should use BFS).

comparisons.note: Rat in a Maze (Backtracking) finds *a* path; BFS finds the *shortest* path.

definition_short: Subgroups of nodes in a graph where every node can reach every other node.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a world with 10 islands. Within one island, you can drive to any house. But you can't drive from Island 1 to Island 2. Each island is a 'Connected Component'. It's a group of points that are all joined together.
real_world_example: Finding all the different 'friend groups' in a school where students only know people in their own group.
technical_definition: A maximal subgraph in which any two vertices are connected to each other by paths.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a disconnected graph with 3 distinct connected components]
common_misconceptions: The whole graph must be connected. (False: A graph can be made of many separate connected components).

comparisons.note: Connected Components are for undirected graphs; Strongly Connected are for directed.

definition_short: Determining if a graph contains any paths that lead back to the starting node.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Loop' test. You want to know if someone could get stuck walking in a circle forever. If you are searching a graph and you find a road that leads back to your own 'Grandparent', you've found a cycle.
real_world_example: Detecting a 'Circular Reference' in an Excel formula where Cell A depends on B, and B depends on A.
technical_definition: The process of identifying cycles in a graph, typically using DFS (back edges) or BFS (Kahn's algorithm).
syntax_or_example: if(visited[neighbor] == true && neighbor != parent) return cycle_detected;
common_misconceptions: Cycles are bad. (False: They are just a feature. However, they break certain algorithms like Topological Sort).

comparisons.note: Finding a Back Edge is the most common way to do Cycle Detection.

definition_short: A BFS-based algorithm for Topological Sorting and Cycle Detection.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'No-Prerequisites' method. You look for any task that has zero requirements and do it. Then you remove that task, which might free up new tasks. You keep doing this until all tasks are done. If tasks are left but they all have requirements, there's a loop (cycle)!
real_world_example: Picking which college courses to take based on which ones don't have any prerequisites left.
technical_definition: An algorithm for topological sorting of a DAG that repeatedly removes vertices with in-degree 0.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Kahn's algorithm step-by-step removing nodes with 0 in-degree]
common_misconceptions: It is only for sorting. (False: It's also one of the best ways to prove if a directed graph has a cycle or not).

comparisons.note: Topological Ordering is the result; Kahn's is the algorithm to get it.

definition_short: Finding the path with the maximum total length in a one-way graph with no loops.
explanation: Simple Explanation: In a one-way street system, what's the longest trip you can take without repeating any city? While finding the longest path is normally 'impossible' for computers, it is very easy and fast if the graph has no loops (DAG).
real_world_example: Calculating the total time a project will take by finding the longest sequence of dependent tasks (the Critical Path).
technical_definition: The problem of finding a path of maximum length in a Directed Acyclic Graph, solvable in O(V+E) using topological sorting and dynamic programming.
syntax_or_example: dist[v] = max(dist[v], dist[u] + weight(u,v));
common_misconceptions: It is as easy as Shortest Path. (False: For general graphs with loops, finding the longest path is extremely hard (NP-Hard)).

comparisons.note: In a DAG, both are solved similarly; in general graphs, Longest Path is much harder.

definition_short: Finding the quickest path in a Directed Acyclic Graph.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you have a one-way system with no loops, you can find the shortest path even faster than Dijkstra! You just sort the cities in order (topological sort) and then check each road once. It even works with negative road costs!
real_world_example: Finding the cheapest way to complete a multi-step manufacturing process.
technical_definition: An O(V+E) algorithm that uses topological sorting to find the shortest distance from a source to all other vertices in a DAG.
syntax_or_example: // 1. Topological Sort; 2. Relax all edges in that order.
common_misconceptions: Dijkstra is the best for this. (False: If you know there are no cycles, the DAG-specific algorithm is faster than Dijkstra).

comparisons.note: DAG shortest path is faster (O(V+E)) and handles negative weights.

definition_short: Finding the shortest distance between every possible pair of nodes in a graph.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is the 'Master Table' of distances. You don't just want the distance from your house to work; you want a giant chart showing the distance from *every* house to *every* other house in the city.
real_world_example: An airline creating a complete price chart for flights between all cities in the world.
technical_definition: The problem of finding shortest paths between all pairs of vertices v, u in a graph.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a distance matrix being filled for all-pairs shortest paths]
common_misconceptions: You should just run Dijkstra from every node. (True for sparse graphs, but for thick graphs, Floyd-Warshall is usually more organized).

comparisons.note: Single Source is one-to-all; All Pairs is all-to-all.

definition_short: Finding the shortest distance from one specific 'starting' node to all other nodes.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You pick one 'Home' node. The algorithm tells you the quickest way to get from Home to any other spot on the map. It doesn't tell you the distance between two *other* spots directly.
real_world_example: Google Maps showing the time to reach different destinations from your current location.
technical_definition: The problem of finding shortest paths from a source vertex v to all other vertices in the graph.
syntax_or_example: // Result: dist[0]=0, dist[1]=5, dist[2]=12...
common_misconceptions: It only finds one path. (False: It finds the best path to *every* other node in the graph simultaneously).

comparisons.note: Single Source is much faster (O(E log V)) than All Pairs (O(V³)).

definition_short: The most famous algorithm for finding the shortest path in weighted graphs.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It works like a 'GPS'. You start at the source and keep a list of the shortest distances you've found so far. You always visit the 'closest' city next and update its neighbors. It's super fast but only works if all roads have positive costs.
real_world_example: Finding the fastest driving route between two cities.
technical_definition: A greedy algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights.
common_misconceptions: It works with negative weights. (False: Negative weights can cause Dijkstra to get stuck or give wrong answers; use Bellman-Ford instead).

comparisons.note: Dijkstra is faster; Bellman-Ford handles negative weights.

definition_short: A shortest path algorithm that can handle negative weights and detect negative cycles.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s a 'Super-Safe' Dijkstra. It is slower, but it works even if some roads have 'negative' costs (like a refund or bonus). It also warns you if there is an infinite loop that makes everything free (Negative Cycle).
real_world_example: Financial systems looking for 'currency loops' where you can trade money back and forth to make a profit (arbitrage).
technical_definition: An algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all of the other vertices in a weighted digraph, allowing negative edge weights.
syntax_or_example: for(i=0; i<V-1; i++) for(edge : edges) relax(edge);
common_misconceptions: It is always better than Dijkstra. (False: It is much slower, so only use it if your graph has negative numbers).

comparisons.note: Bellman-Ford is O(V*E); Dijkstra is O(E log V).

definition_short: An algorithm for finding shortest paths between all pairs of vertices.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This algorithm uses 'Dynamic Programming'. It asks: 'Can I get from A to B faster if I stop at City K in the middle?'. It repeats this for every possible middle city until the whole distance table is perfectly solved.
real_world_example: A logistics company calculating the cheapest shipping route between all 50 warehouses in a country.
technical_definition: An O(V³) algorithm that calculates the shortest paths between all pairs of nodes using a triple-nested loop structure.
syntax_or_example: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]);
common_misconceptions: It is for large graphs. (False: Because it's O(V³), it is only good for graphs with a few hundred nodes; any more and it becomes way too slow).

comparisons.note: Floyd-Warshall is better for 'Dense' (many roads) graphs; Johnson's is better for 'Sparse' graphs.

definition_short: An algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths optimized for sparse graphs.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s a 'Hybrid' trick. It uses Bellman-Ford once to 're-color' the graph so all roads become positive. Then, it can safely use the lightning-fast Dijkstra algorithm for every city. It is the best choice if your map has few roads but many cities.
real_world_example: Calculating all-to-all distances in a nationwide fiber-optic network where cities only connect to their neighbors.
technical_definition: An algorithm that finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a sparse weighted directed graph in O(V² log V + VE) time.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Johnson's algorithm reweighting edges to make them non-negative]
common_misconceptions: It is simpler than Floyd-Warshall. (False: It is much more complex to code because it involves two different algorithms and a reweighting step).

comparisons.note: Johnson's is faster for Sparse graphs; Floyd-Warshall is better for Dense graphs.

definition_short: A pathfinding algorithm that uses 'Heuristics' to find the shortest path faster than Dijkstra.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s 'Dijkstra with a Brain'. Dijkstra searches in every direction equally. A* uses a 'Heuristic' (a smart guess, like 'straight-line distance to the goal') to focus its search in the right direction, saving a huge amount of time.
real_world_example: The pathfinding logic for characters in video games (like moving an army in Age of Empires).
technical_definition: An extension of Dijkstra's algorithm that uses the cost function f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g is the path cost and h is an estimated cost to the goal.
syntax_or_example: [Image comparing Dijkstra's wide search vs A* directed search]\nf = g + h;
common_misconceptions: It always finds the shortest path. (True, but ONLY if your heuristic 'h' is 'admissible', meaning it never overestimates the distance).

comparisons.note: Dijkstra is a specific case of A* where the heuristic is zero.

definition_short: An algorithm that makes the best local choice at each step hoping to find the global optimum.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Take the Best Now' rule. You don't think about the long-term future; you just pick the most profitable or cheapest option available right this second. It's very fast, but it doesn't always find the perfect final answer.
real_world_example: Counting change: you always use the largest coin possible first ($50, then $20, then $10).
technical_definition: An algorithmic paradigm that follows the problem-solving heuristic of making the locally optimal choice at each stage with the intent of finding a global optimum.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a Greedy approach picking the highest value at each junction]
common_misconceptions: Greedy always works. (False: In many problems, like the 0/1 Knapsack, being greedy now can lead to a terrible result later).

comparisons.note: Greedy is fast but can be wrong; DP is slower but always finds the perfect answer.

definition_short: The fundamental property of Greedy algorithms where a local best choice leads to a global solution.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is the 'No Regrets' property. If you can prove that picking the best thing *right now* will never come back to haunt you later, then you can use a Greedy Algorithm. If you might regret it, you can't be greedy.
real_world_example: If you are hungry and want to eat the most calories, and all food is free, picking the biggest burger is a safe greedy choice.
technical_definition: The property that a global optimum can be reached by choosing a local optimum.
syntax_or_example: best_now = max(available_options); result += best_now;
common_misconceptions: Every choice is a greedy choice. (False: A choice is only 'greedy' if it is determined purely by the current best value without looking ahead).

comparisons.note: Optimal Substructure is needed for DP; Greedy Choice is the extra rule needed for Greedy.

definition_short: A greedy algorithm used for lossless data compression.
explanation: Simple Explanation: How do we make a file smaller? We give short codes to letters that appear often (like 'E' and 'A') and long codes to letters that are rare (like 'Z' and 'Q'). This 'Greedy' way of assigning codes saves a lot of space.
real_world_example: ZIP files and JPEG images use Huffman coding to reduce file size.
technical_definition: An algorithm that uses a frequency-based binary tree to assign variable-length prefix codes to characters.
common_misconceptions: It loses data. (False: It is 'Lossless', meaning you get 100% of the original data back when you unzip it).

comparisons.note: Huffman (variable-length) is much smaller than standard ASCII (fixed-length).

definition_short: A greedy algorithm that finds the Minimum Spanning Tree by sorting edges.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You look at every road in the world and sort them by price. You buy the cheapest road first. If it connects two cities that aren't already connected, keep it. If it creates a loop, throw it away. Repeat until everyone is connected.
real_world_example: A cable company laying internet lines between 50 cities using the least amount of cable possible.
technical_definition: A greedy algorithm that finds a minimum spanning tree for a connected weighted graph by sorting edges and using Disjoint Set Union to avoid cycles.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Kruskal's algorithm: adding sorted edges to form a tree]
common_misconceptions: It is always faster than Prim's. (False: For very 'dense' graphs with millions of edges, Prim's is often better).

comparisons.note: Kruskal's grows a 'Forest' of edges; Prim's grows one single 'Tree'.

definition_short: A greedy algorithm that finds the Minimum Spanning Tree by growing a tree from a source.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You start at one city and look at all the roads leading out. You pick the cheapest road to a new city. Now you have two cities; you look at all roads leading out of *both* of them and pick the cheapest one to a third city. You keep 'growing' the tree outward.
real_world_example: Connecting a new office building to the power grid by starting at the main station and taking the shortest path to each new room.
technical_definition: A greedy algorithm that builds an MST by selecting the minimum weight edge that connects a vertex in the tree to a vertex outside it.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Prim's algorithm growing a tree level by level]
common_misconceptions: It handles disconnected graphs. (False: Prim's only works on connected graphs; Kruskal's is better if your graph is broken into pieces).

comparisons.note: Prim's is better for 'Dense' graphs; Kruskal's is better for 'Sparse' graphs.

definition_short: A greedy algorithm to complete the most profitable jobs within their deadlines.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have 10 jobs, each with a deadline and a profit. You can only do one job at a time. To make the most money, you sort the jobs by profit and try to fit the most expensive ones into the latest possible time slots before they expire.
real_world_example: A freelancer picking which client projects to work on today to make the most money without missing any deadlines.
technical_definition: A scheduling problem where each job has a deadline and a profit, solved greedily by sorting jobs in descending order of profit.
syntax_or_example: Sort by Profit: [Job A: $100, Job B: $50...] -> Fill slots.
common_misconceptions: You should do the earliest deadline first. (False: That's a different problem. For max profit, you do the most *valuable* jobs first, even if their deadlines are later).

comparisons.note: Activity Selection is about doing the *most* jobs; Job Sequencing is about the most *profit*.

definition_short: A greedy algorithm for the knapsack problem where you can take pieces of items.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are a thief with a bag that holds 10kg. You find gold dust, silver dust, and copper dust. To get the most money, you calculate the 'Price per kg' and fill your bag with the most expensive dust first. If the bag gets full, you just take a small scoop of the next item.
real_world_example: Mixing ingredients for a recipe to get the best taste for the lowest total cost.
technical_definition: A knapsack variant where items can be divided, solved by taking items in descending order of their value-to-weight ratio.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Fractional Knapsack: taking full items then a fraction of the last one]\nSort by: (Value / Weight);
common_misconceptions: It works for whole items (like laptops). (False: This only works for things you can cut, like gold dust or liquids; for whole items, you need '0/1 Knapsack').

comparisons.note: Fractional is Greedy (fast); 0/1 is Dynamic Programming (slower but handles whole items).

definition_short: A greedy algorithm to find the maximum number of non-overlapping activities.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have a meeting room and 10 requests for meetings at different times. To fit the *most* meetings, you always pick the meeting that finishes the earliest. This leaves the most time possible for future meetings.
real_world_example: A theater manager scheduling as many movie screenings as possible in one single theater.
technical_definition: A problem of selecting the maximum number of activities that can be performed by a single person or machine, solved by sorting by finish time.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing non-overlapping time blocks selected by earliest finish time]\nSort by: finish_time;
common_misconceptions: You should pick the shortest meeting. (False: Picking a short meeting in the middle of the day might block two other important meetings; picking the earliest finisher is always better).

comparisons.note: Activity Selection cares about the *count* of tasks; Job Sequencing cares about the *money*.

definition_short: Using a greedy approach to find the minimum number of coins for a given amount.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you need to give $70 change, you give a $50 bill, then a $20 bill. You always take the largest coin possible first. This works perfectly for US Dollars or Indian Rupees, but it doesn't work for all possible coin systems!
real_world_example: A cashier or vending machine giving you change in the fewest coins possible.
technical_definition: A greedy strategy for the coin change problem that repeatedly selects the largest denomination less than the remaining amount.
syntax_or_example: while(amount >= coin) { amount -= coin; count++; }
common_misconceptions: It always gives the best answer. (False: If your coins are {1, 3, 4} and you need 6, Greedy gives {4, 1, 1} = 3 coins, but the best answer is {3, 3} = 2 coins. For those cases, you need DP).

comparisons.note: Greedy is for 'standard' coin systems; DP is for *any* coin system.

definition_short: A property where a large problem is broken into smaller pieces that are solved many times.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine calculating your family tree. To find your grandfather's birthday, you ask your dad. To find your great-uncle's birthday, you ask your dad again. You keep asking for the same info! In DP, we save that info (Memoization) so we only ask once.
real_world_example: Calculating Fibonacci numbers: to find Fib(10), you have to calculate Fib(5) dozens of different times.
technical_definition: A situation where a recursive algorithm visits the same subproblems repeatedly with the same input values.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a recursion tree where many nodes are the same color/value]
common_misconceptions: It's the same as 'Divide and Conquer'. (False: Divide and Conquer splits into *unique* pieces; DP handles *repeated* pieces).

comparisons.note: Overlapping Subproblems means we should *save* answers; Optimal Substructure means we *can* build the answer from pieces.

definition_short: Finding the longest sequence of characters that appears in two different strings in the same order.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you have 'ABCDE' and 'ACEB', the LCS is 'ACE'. The letters don't have to be right next to each other, but they must be in the same order. It’s used to find how similar two things are.
real_world_example: Comparing two versions of a document to highlight what changed (like 'Diff' in Git).
technical_definition: A dynamic programming problem that finds the longest sequence that can be derived from two strings by deleting some or no characters.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing an LCS table comparing two strings]\nif(s1[i] == s2[j]) dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1];
common_misconceptions: It's the same as a Substring. (False: Substrings must be 'contiguous' (connected); Subsequences can have gaps).

comparisons.note: LCS finds what is the *same*; Edit Distance finds how much is *different*.

definition_short: Finding the longest possible subsequence where the numbers are in strictly increasing order.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you have [10, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101], the LIS is [2, 3, 7, 101]. You want to pick the longest 'growing' chain of numbers from the list, even if you have to skip some in the middle.
real_world_example: Tracking a company's stock price and finding the longest period of overall growth, even if there were some bad days.
technical_definition: The problem of finding a subsequence of a given sequence in which the subsequence's elements are in sorted order, lowest to highest.
syntax_or_example: for(i=0; i<n; i++) for(j=0; j<i; j++) if(a[i] > a[j]) dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1);
common_misconceptions: It's O(n²). (True for basic DP, but there is a very clever 'Patient Sorting' trick that makes it O(n log n)).

comparisons.note: Sorting changes the list; LIS finds the best 'hidden' order within the existing list.

definition_short: A knapsack problem where you must either take a whole item or leave it completely.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You are a thief with a 10kg bag. You find a TV, a Laptop, and a Microwave. You can't take 'half a TV'. You have to choose the combination of whole items that gives you the most money without breaking your bag.
real_world_example: Budgeting: You have $1000 and 5 projects you could fund. You either fund a project completely or not at all.
technical_definition: A classic DP problem where given weights and values of n items, you find the maximum value you can fit in a capacity W by deciding (0 or 1) for each item.
syntax_or_example: dp[i][w] = max(val[i] + dp[i-1][w-wt[i]], dp[i-1][w]);
common_misconceptions: Greedy works for this. (False: Being greedy (taking most valuable first) can fill your bag too early and leave no room for better combinations later).

comparisons.note: Fractional allows 'scooping' pieces (Greedy); 0/1 requires 'whole' items (DP).

definition_short: Finding the most efficient way to multiply a sequence of matrices.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Multiplying matrices (A*B*C) always gives the same result, but the *order* you do it in changes how many billions of math operations the computer has to do. This DP algorithm finds the exact 'Parentheses' (A*(B*C)) to make it finish as fast as possible.
real_world_example: A compiler deciding which order to process complex graphics equations to save battery on your phone.
technical_definition: A problem that finds the most efficient way to multiply a given sequence of matrices by minimizing the number of scalar multiplications.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing different parenthesization of matrices and their math costs]
common_misconceptions: It actually multiplies the matrices. (False: It only calculates the *plan* for which ones to multiply first; it doesn't do the actual math).

comparisons.note: A greedy approach (multiplying the smallest first) can often be wrong; DP always finds the perfect order.

definition_short: The minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace) to turn one string into another.
explanation: Simple Explanation: How many typos are in 'Kushh' vs 'Kush'? Just one (delete 'h'). Edit distance is the 'Fix-it' score. It tells you how many steps it takes to change 'Apple' into 'Apply'.
real_world_example: Auto-correct suggestions: the computer suggests words with the smallest edit distance to your typo.
technical_definition: Also called Levenshtein Distance; a DP algorithm that calculates the transformation cost between two strings.
common_misconceptions: It only counts spelling mistakes. (False: It’s used in DNA analysis to see how many mutations happened between two species).

comparisons.note: LCS counts similarity; Edit Distance counts the 'work' to change one to another.

definition_short: Finding the best way to cut a long rod into smaller pieces to maximize profit.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have a 4m rod. You can sell a 4m piece for $9, or two 2m pieces for $10. Rod cutting uses DP to look at all possible cuts and find the one that puts the most money in your pocket.
real_world_example: A lumberjack cutting a tree trunk into different lengths of boards based on current market prices.
technical_definition: A classic DP problem where given a rod of length n and a list of prices for each length, you find the maximum revenue obtainable by cutting the rod.
syntax_or_example: max_val = max(price[i] + cutRod(n-i-1));
common_misconceptions: Cutting more pieces is always better. (False: Sometimes the labor/price of small pieces isn't worth it; one long piece might be more valuable).

comparisons.note: Rod Cutting is essentially the Unbounded Knapsack problem (you can take as many pieces of one length as you want).

definition_short: A DP algorithm to find the minimum coins for *any* possible coin system.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Remember how Greedy fails if coins are {1, 3, 4}? The DP version creates a table and calculates the best answer for $1, then $2, then $3... using the previous answers. It takes longer than Greedy but it is *never* wrong.
real_world_example: Designing a new currency for a video game where you need to guarantee that players can always use the fewest coins possible.
technical_definition: A dynamic programming approach that finds the minimum number of coins to make a change for a value V using a given set of denominations.
syntax_or_example: dp[i] = min(dp[i], 1 + dp[i - coins[j]]);
common_misconceptions: It is only for 'minimum coins'. (False: A variation of this same DP can also tell you 'how many different ways' you can make change).

comparisons.note: Greedy is O(coins); DP is O(amount * coins). DP is slower but always accurate.

definition_short: Deciding if a set of numbers can be split into two groups with equal sums.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have 10 weights. Can you put them into two boxes so that both boxes weigh exactly the same? This is a 'Yes/No' question that DP solves by checking if it's possible to reach 'Total Sum / 2'.
real_world_example: Dividing a group of tasks between two workers so they both finish at the exact same time.
technical_definition: A task of determining whether a given multiset of positive integers can be partitioned into two subsets S1 and S2 such that the sum of elements in S1 equals the sum of elements in S2.
syntax_or_example: if(sum % 2 != 0) return false; // Basic check.
common_misconceptions: It is easy for large sets. (False: This is an 'NP-Complete' problem, meaning as you add more numbers, it becomes extremely difficult for the computer to check every possibility).

comparisons.note: The Partition Problem is a special case of the Subset Sum problem where the target is exactly half the total.

definition_short: A problem of finding if any subset of numbers in a set adds up exactly to a target value.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a bunch of different weights and a scale. You want to know if you can pick a specific combination of those weights to reach exactly 50kg. It's like a puzzle where you decide 'yes' or 'no' for each number to reach a goal sum.
real_world_example: A shopper trying to use exact change from a pocket full of random coins to pay a specific bill amount.
technical_definition: A decision problem in computer science where, given a multiset of integers and a target sum T, the goal is to determine if there exists a non-empty subset whose elements sum to T.
syntax_or_example: \ndp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] || dp[i-1][j - arr[i]];
common_misconceptions: It can be solved by just picking the largest numbers. (False: This greedy approach fails; you must explore combinations using DP or recursion).

comparisons.note: Partition Problem is a special case where the target sum is exactly half of the total sum.

definition_short: A fundamental equation used in Dynamic Programming to break a decision problem into smaller subproblems.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It's the 'Master Rule' for solving big problems. It says: 'The best way to solve this big problem is to make one best choice now, and then add it to the best solution for the remaining smaller problem.' It is the heart of how computers 'learn' the best path.
real_world_example: A GPS calculating the fastest route: the best way from A to C is the best way from A to B plus the best way from B to C.
technical_definition: An equation that expresses the value of a decision problem at a certain point in time in terms of the payoff from initial choices and the value of the remaining decision problem.
syntax_or_example: V(s) = max(R(s, a) + γV(s')) // Value = Reward + Future Value.
common_misconceptions: It is only for Reinforcement Learning. (False: It is the mathematical foundation for almost all Dynamic Programming algorithms like Shortest Path).

comparisons.note: The Bellman Equation provides the logical rule that a recursive function follows to be optimal.

definition_short: A BST designed to minimize the total search time based on how often each key is searched.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a library where some books are borrowed every day and others once a year. An 'Optimal' tree puts the popular books near the root (top) so they are found faster, even if the tree looks lopsided. It's about 'Search Efficiency'.
real_world_example: A translator app keeping the most common words (like 'the', 'is') at the top of its search tree to save processing power.
technical_definition: A BST where the expected cost of searching (sum of probability * depth) is minimized for a given set of keys and their access frequencies.
syntax_or_example: \nCost(i, j) = weight(i, j) + min(Cost(i, k-1) + Cost(k+1, j));
common_misconceptions: It is the same as an AVL tree. (False: AVL focuses on height balance; Optimal BST focuses on access frequency balance).

comparisons.note: Both use frequency to optimize access, but Huffman is for compression and Optimal BST is for searching.

definition_short: Using Dynamic Programming to find all-pairs shortest paths by considering every node as an intermediate step.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It's like building a giant map of shortcuts. You start with direct roads. Then you ask: 'Can I get from A to B faster if I pass through City 1?'. Then you ask about City 2, then City 3. By the end, you've checked every possible shortcut.
real_world_example: A logistics company updating their entire delivery database to find the cheapest route between every single pair of warehouses.
technical_definition: An O(V³) algorithm that uses a DP state dp[k][i][j] representing the shortest path from i to j using only vertices from {1..k} as intermediates.
syntax_or_example: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]);
common_misconceptions: It is faster than Dijkstra. (False: It's slower for one path, but it's more organized if you need *every* path between *every* city).

comparisons.note: Dijkstra finds paths from one start point; Floyd-Warshall finds paths between all points.

definition_short: A DP technique that uses binary 'bits' to represent a set of items or states.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have 5 light switches. Instead of listing every switch as 'On' or 'Off', you use a 5-digit binary number like '10110'. This number is a 'mask'. It lets you represent complex 'groups' of items using a single small integer, which makes the code very fast.
real_world_example: A travel app finding the best route to visit 10 specific cities—it uses bits to remember which cities it has already visited.
technical_definition: A dynamic programming approach where a small integer's binary representation is used to represent a subset of elements, typically for problems involving permutations or subsets.
syntax_or_example: if (mask & (1 << i)) // Check if the i-th item is in the set.
common_misconceptions: It can be used for any number of items. (False: It only works for small sets (usually up to 20-25 items) because the number of states doubles with every new item).

comparisons.note: Subset Sum looks for a sum; Bitmask DP usually looks for the best *order* or *combination* of specific items.

definition_short: A DP technique used to count numbers with specific properties within a range [L, R].
explanation: Simple Explanation: How many numbers between 1 and 1,000,000 have the digit '7' in them? Instead of checking every number (which is slow), Digit DP builds the numbers digit by digit (thousands place, hundreds place...) and counts the valid combinations. It's much faster than counting by hand.
real_world_example: A bank counting how many account numbers follow a specific security pattern within a certain range.
technical_definition: A DP approach that processes numbers as strings of digits to solve counting problems over a large range of integers, typically using state (index, tight_constraint, current_property).
syntax_or_example: solve(index, is_less, has_seven);
common_misconceptions: It is for simple math. (False: It's for problems that are too big for a loop, usually involving ranges up to 10^18).

comparisons.note: Brute force takes O(N) time; Digit DP takes O(log N) time (based on the number of digits).

definition_short: Applying Dynamic Programming on a tree structure, usually using recursion.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You solve a problem for the 'leaves' (bottom nodes) first. Then, you use those answers to solve the problem for the 'parents', working your way up to the root. It’s like calculating the budget for a whole company by starting with the smallest departments.
real_world_example: Finding the longest path in a family tree or the most efficient way to place security guards in a building with branches.
technical_definition: A DP technique where the subproblems are defined by the subtrees of a given tree, typically solved using a Post-order traversal (Bottom-up).
syntax_or_example: dp[u] = val[u] + sum(dp[v]) for all children v;
common_misconceptions: It only works on binary trees. (False: It works on any tree structure by iterating through the children of each node).

comparisons.note: Tree DP is easier because trees have no cycles and a clear parent-child direction.

definition_short: Finding the shortest path by breaking the problem into subproblems based on distances.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To find the shortest path from A to Z, you first find the shortest path from A to Z's neighbors. It's the 'Build-a-Route' method where you use previous distance calculations to find new ones. Bellman-Ford and Floyd-Warshall are both Shortest Path DP algorithms.
real_world_example: Finding the cheapest multi-stop flight by looking at the cheapest flights to each layover city first.
technical_definition: The use of DP (like the Bellman equation) to compute shortest distances, typically relying on optimal substructure where a sub-path of a shortest path is also a shortest path.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing distance relaxation: if (d[u] + w < d[v]) d[v] = d[u] + w]
common_misconceptions: Dijkstra is always DP. (Technically, Dijkstra is Greedy, while Bellman-Ford and Floyd-Warshall are true DP because they handle all possibilities or negative weights).

comparisons.note: Greedy picks the best *immediate* step; DP considers the best *overall* combination.

definition_short: Finding one or more occurrences of a 'pattern' string inside a larger 'text' string.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Find' or 'Search' feature. You have a long book (text) and you want to find every time the word 'Kush' (pattern) appears. The computer scans the book to find a match.
real_world_example: Pressing Ctrl+F in a PDF to find a specific sentence.
technical_definition: The problem of finding all the occurrences of a pattern P in a text T, both being strings over some alphabet.
syntax_or_example: // Text: 'banana', Pattern: 'ana' -> Found at index 1 and 3.
common_misconceptions: It's just a simple loop. (False: Simple loops are very slow for large texts; advanced algorithms like KMP are used for speed).

comparisons.note: String matching is exact; Pattern recognition (like AI) can be fuzzy or approximate.

definition_short: A simple string matching algorithm that checks every possible position one by one.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It's the 'Brute Force' way. You hold your pattern up to the start of the text. If it doesn't match, you slide it forward by exactly one letter and check again. You keep sliding and checking until the end. It's easy but slow.
real_world_example: Looking for a name in a physical list by putting your finger on each line and reading it letter by letter.
technical_definition: The simplest string matching algorithm with O(N*M) complexity, where N is text length and M is pattern length.
common_misconceptions: It is never used. (False: It is actually fine for very short texts or patterns where fancy algorithms are overkill).

comparisons.note: Naive slides by 1 letter; KMP slides by many letters using a 'prefix' table.

definition_short: Knuth-Morris-Pratt; a fast string search that never looks at a character more than once.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s 'Search with Memory'. If you are looking for 'ABCABCY' and you fail at 'Y', you don't go back to the beginning! You know you just saw 'ABCABC', so you jump ahead and start checking from the second 'ABC'. It saves time by not repeating work.
real_world_example: A music app finding a specific beat pattern in a song without restarting from the beginning of the track every time it misses a note.
technical_definition: An O(N+M) string matching algorithm that uses a 'Partial Match' table (LPS array) to determine how much to skip after a mismatch.
syntax_or_example: \n// Uses the 'Longest Prefix which is also a Suffix'.
common_misconceptions: It is the same as Rabin-Karp. (False: KMP uses prefix patterns; Rabin-Karp uses math/hashing).

comparisons.note: KMP is much faster (O(N)) because it never 'backtracks' the text pointer.

definition_short: A string search algorithm that uses hashing to find patterns.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Instead of comparing letters, it turns the pattern into a number (a hash). Then it turns chunks of the text into numbers too. If the numbers match, the words probably match! It uses 'Rolling Hashes' to slide through the text very fast.
real_world_example: Plagiarism detectors: checking if a whole sentence from your essay matches a sentence on the internet by comparing 'sentence fingerprints'.
technical_definition: A string matching algorithm that uses hashing to find any one of a set of pattern strings in a text in O(N+M) average time.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a rolling hash sliding across a string]\nif(hash_p == hash_t) check_actual_match();
common_misconceptions: It is 100% accurate with just hashing. (False: Two different strings can have the same hash (collision). You must always do one final letter-by-letter check if the hashes match).

comparisons.note: Rabin-Karp is better for finding *multiple* different patterns at once.

definition_short: The standard string search used in 'grep' and text editors; it searches from right to left.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Backward Search'. It looks at the *last* letter of the pattern first. If that letter doesn't even exist in your word, you can skip the *entire* pattern length ahead! It is usually the fastest algorithm for real-world text searching.
real_world_example: The 'Search' function in VS Code or Notepad++.
technical_definition: An efficient string searching algorithm that uses two heuristics (Bad Character and Good Suffix) to skip as much of the text as possible.
syntax_or_example: \n// Best case: O(N/M).
common_misconceptions: It searches the text backward. (False: It slides through the text forward, but it compares the pattern letters starting from the end of the pattern).

comparisons.note: Boyer-Moore is usually faster in practice because it can skip many characters entirely.

definition_short: A string matching method that uses a 'State Machine' to track progress.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a game board with 5 spots. Every time you see the right letter, you move forward one spot. If you see a wrong letter, the board tells you exactly which spot to 'fall back' to. When you reach the finish line, you found the word.
technical_definition: A string matching algorithm that builds a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) where each state represents a prefix of the pattern.
common_misconceptions: It's the same as KMP. (False: They follow similar logic, but Finite Automata are pre-built to handle *any* letter instantly, making the search phase slightly faster but the setup slower).

comparisons.note: KMP is easier to build; Finite Automata are faster to run if you have a small alphabet.

definition_short: An algorithm that searches for multiple different words in a text at the same time.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine looking for 'Apple', 'Banana', and 'Cherry' in a book. Instead of reading the book 3 times, you build a special 'Word Tree' (Trie) with shortcuts. You read the book once, and the tree tells you every time you hit *any* of the words.
real_world_example: An antivirus program scanning a file for 10,000 different known virus 'signatures' at once.
technical_definition: A dictionary-matching algorithm that locates elements of a finite set of strings (the dictionary) within an input text by using a trie with failure links.
common_misconceptions: It is only for single words. (False: Its main power is finding many words simultaneously in one pass).

comparisons.note: Aho-Corasick is a Trie with extra 'failure links' that let you jump between branches.

definition_short: An algorithm that finds all occurrences of a pattern by calculating 'Z-values' for every position.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s a 'Self-Similarity' map. For every spot in a string, it calculates: 'How long is the prefix starting here that also matches the start of the string?'. By looking at this map, you can instantly see where your pattern appears.
real_world_example: Finding all repeating patterns in a DNA sequence to identify similar genes.
technical_definition: An O(N+M) algorithm that produces a Z-array where Z[i] is the length of the longest common prefix between the string and the suffix starting at i.
syntax_or_example: String: 'aabcaabx' -> Z[4]=3 (because 'aab' repeats at index 4).
common_misconceptions: It is harder than KMP. (False: Many find the Z-algorithm logic more intuitive and easier to code than KMP's LPS array).

comparisons.note: Both are O(N+M), but Z-algorithm is often used in different string problems like finding period of a string.

definition_short: A very fast algorithm to find the longest palindromic substring in O(N) time.
explanation: Simple Explanation: A palindrome reads the same backward (like 'RACECAR'). Usually, finding the longest one is slow. Manacher's uses the symmetry of palindromes—if there's a palindrome at the left side, there's likely one at the right side too. It skips the work by 'mirroring' results.
real_world_example: Analyzing genetic sequences (which often have palindromic patterns) to find important biological markers.
technical_definition: An O(N) algorithm that finds the longest palindrome centered at each position by utilizing previously computed palindrome lengths and symmetry.
common_misconceptions: It only works for odd-length strings. (False: It uses a trick of adding special characters like '#' between letters to make every palindrome 'odd' and easy to process).

comparisons.note: Brute force takes O(N²); Manacher's takes O(N).

definition_short: A state machine that represents every single substring of a given string.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Ultimate String Tool'. It’s a very compact map that contains *every* possible small word you could find inside a big word. It can answer almost any question about a string (count repeats, find longest common part) instantly.
real_world_example: High-end data compression or advanced search tools in bioinformatics.
technical_definition: A minimal deterministic finite automaton that accepts all suffixes of a given string, capable of solving complex string problems in O(N) time.
common_misconceptions: It's the same as a Suffix Tree. (False: They do similar things, but the Automaton is more memory-efficient and often faster to build).

comparisons.note: Suffix Tree is for 'paths'; Suffix Automaton is for 'states'.

definition_short: Algorithms designed to solve mathematical problems like factors, primes, or equations.
explanation: Simple Explanation: These are the 'Calculator' algorithms. They aren't about sorting lists or searching strings; they are about finding the Greatest Common Divisor, checking if a number is Prime, or doing complex division very fast.
real_world_example: The math behind credit card encryption or calculating the path of a satellite.
technical_definition: A procedure for solving a mathematical problem (as of finding a greatest common divisor) in a finite number of steps.
syntax_or_example: // Examples: Sieve, Euclid's, Miller-Rabin.
common_misconceptions: Computer math is always perfect. (False: Computers have limits on how big a number can be (precision), so we need special algorithms for huge numbers).

comparisons.note: Mathematical algorithms usually give an exact, proven answer; Heuristics give a 'good enough' guess.

definition_short: An ancient and fast way to find the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two numbers.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To find the GCD of 100 and 40, you subtract 40 from 100 until you can't anymore. (100-40-40 = 20). Then you do the same with 40 and 20. (40-20-20 = 0). The last number that fit perfectly (20) is your answer. It's much faster than listing all factors.
real_world_example: A tile layer finding the largest square tile that can perfectly cover a rectangular room without any gaps.
technical_definition: An iterative method to compute the GCD by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of the larger divided by the smaller (a % b) until the remainder is zero.
syntax_or_example: int gcd(a, b) { return b == 0 ? a : gcd(b, a % b); }
common_misconceptions: It is slow for huge numbers. (False: Even for numbers with hundreds of digits, this algorithm is incredibly fast (Logarithmic time)).

comparisons.note: Listing all factors is O(√N); Euclidean algorithm is O(log N).

definition_short: A fast way to find all prime numbers up to a certain limit.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a grid of numbers from 1 to 100. 1. Circle '2' and cross out all multiples of 2. 2. Circle '3' and cross out all multiples of 3. 3. Keep going to the next 'not-crossed' number. When you're done, only the Primes are left circled.
real_world_example: Generating a list of prime numbers for security keys used in online banking.
technical_definition: An O(N log log N) algorithm for finding all primes up to n by iteratively marking the multiples of each prime starting from 2.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing numbers 1-100 with multiples being crossed out in different colors]\nif (isPrime[p]) for (i = p*p; i <= n; i += p) isPrime[i] = false;
common_misconceptions: It is for checking one number. (False: It's slow for one number; its power is in finding *every* prime in a large range at once).

comparisons.note: Sieve finds a *range* of primes; Primality Test checks *one* number.

definition_short: Greatest Common Divisor; the largest number that divides two numbers perfectly.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Biggest Shared Piece'. For 12 and 18, the GCD is 6. Why? Because 6 is the largest number that can 'fit' inside both 12 and 18 with nothing left over.
real_world_example: Simplifying fractions: in 12/18, you divide both by the GCD (6) to get the simplest form: 2/3.
technical_definition: The greatest common divisor of two or more integers, which are not all zero, is the largest positive integer that divides each of the integers.
common_misconceptions: GCD must be one of the two numbers. (False: It can be smaller than both, though it can't be bigger than either).

comparisons.note: GCD is the largest *divisor*; LCM is the smallest *multiple*.

definition_short: Least Common Multiple; the smallest number that both input numbers can divide into.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'First Meeting Point'. If one bell rings every 4 minutes and another every 6 minutes, when will they ring together? At 12 minutes (the LCM). 12 is the first number that both 4 and 6 hit.
real_world_example: Planning a party: if hotdogs come in packs of 8 and buns in packs of 12, you need 24 (the LCM) of each to have zero leftovers.
technical_definition: The smallest positive integer that is divisible by both a and b.
syntax_or_example: LCM(a, b) = (a * b) / GCD(a, b);
common_misconceptions: LCM is always (a * b). (False: This only happens if they share no factors. If they do, the LCM is smaller than their product).

comparisons.note: LCM is about 'reaching' a number; GCD is about 'splitting' a number.

definition_short: Calculating (A to the power of B) % C very quickly, even for huge numbers.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Calculating 2^1000 is impossible for a normal computer (the number is too big). But calculating (2^1000) % 7 is easy! This algorithm does the math in small steps and keeps the numbers tiny at every stage. It's the secret to internet security.
real_world_example: The math behind 'HTTPS' and SSL certificates that keep your passwords safe when you log in to websites.
technical_definition: A type of exponentiation performed over a modulus, computed in O(log exponent) time using the method of 'binary exponentiation'.
syntax_or_example: res = (res * base) % mod; base = (base * base) % mod;
common_misconceptions: You calculate the power first then use %. (False: The power is way too big; you MUST use the % at every step to keep the numbers small).

comparisons.note: Binary Exponentiation is the 'method'; Modular is adding the '%' rule to that method.

definition_short: An algorithm to determine if a given number is Prime (only divisible by 1 and itself).
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Prime Check'. You want to know if 17 is prime. The simplest way is to try dividing it by 2, 3, 4... up to 16. If none work, it's prime. For huge numbers, we use smarter tricks like Miller-Rabin.
real_world_example: Encryption systems need to find massive prime numbers (hundreds of digits long) to create secure keys.
technical_definition: A test to determine whether an input number is prime, ranging from deterministic (trial division) to probabilistic (Miller-Rabin).
syntax_or_example: // Simple check: for(i=2; i*i <= n; i++) if(n % i == 0) return false;
common_misconceptions: Prime checking is just for math class. (False: Every time you buy something online, your computer performs thousands of primality tests to secure the payment).

comparisons.note: Primality test is for *one* number; Sieve is for *all* numbers in a range.

definition_short: A very fast probabilistic test to check if a large number is prime.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It's a '99.9% Sure' test. For a number with 500 digits, a perfect check is too slow. Miller-Rabin does a few quick 'random experiments'. If the number fails even one, it's definitely not prime. If it passes many, it is almost certainly prime.
real_world_example: The standard tool used by computers to generate prime numbers for RSA encryption.
technical_definition: A probabilistic primality test that determines if a number is a strong pseudoprime to a given base, with an error probability that decreases exponentially with the number of bases tested.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing the Miller-Rabin logic flow: picking a witness and checking conditions]
common_misconceptions: It can give a 'false negative'. (False: If it says a number is 'not prime', it is 100% correct. If it says it 'is prime', there is a tiny, tiny chance it's wrong).

comparisons.note: Trial Division is O(√N) and slow; Miller-Rabin is O(log³ N) and extremely fast.

definition_short: An algorithm used to multiply huge polynomials or process signals (like sound) very fast.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have two 1,000,000-digit numbers to multiply. Normal math is too slow. FFT turns those numbers into 'waves', multiplies the waves simply, and turns them back into numbers. It's also how your phone turns noise into a digital MP3 file.
real_world_example: Digital signal processing: how noise-canceling headphones filter out background sound.
technical_definition: An O(N log N) algorithm to compute the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), converting a signal from the time domain to the frequency domain.
common_misconceptions: It's just for math. (False: It is one of the most important algorithms in history for audio, video, and communication technology).

comparisons.note: Naive takes O(N²); FFT-based multiplication takes O(N log N).

definition_short: A divide-and-conquer algorithm to multiply two matrices faster than the standard method.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Standard matrix math needs 8 multiplications for small blocks. Strassen found a weird trick to do it with only 7. While it sounds small, when you have a matrix with billions of cells, saving that 1 multiplication over and over makes the computer much faster.
real_world_example: High-performance graphics engines in modern video games that need to do massive amounts of matrix math every second.
technical_definition: An algorithm for matrix multiplication that is asymptotically faster than the standard O(n³) algorithm, achieving roughly O(n^2.81) time.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Strassen's 7 sub-multiplication formulas]
common_misconceptions: It is always better. (False: Because the trick is complex, it is only faster for *very* large matrices; for small ones, standard math is actually faster).

comparisons.note: Standard is O(N³); Strassen is O(N^2.81).

definition_short: Algorithms designed to solve problems involving points, lines, and shapes.
explanation: Simple Explanation: These are the 'Blueprint' algorithms. They help a computer understand space. For example: 'What's the shortest distance between these points?' or 'Does this line hit that circle?'. They are essential for maps, games, and robots.
real_world_example: A self-driving car calculating if it can fit into a parking spot based on sensor 'points'.
technical_definition: A class of algorithms that deal with geometric objects and their properties.
syntax_or_example: // Examples: Convex Hull, Line Intersection, Delaunay Triangulation.
common_misconceptions: Geometry is just math. (False: Computational Geometry is a huge part of Computer Science focused on speed and handling messy data).

comparisons.note: Graph algorithms care about 'connections'; Geometric algorithms care about 'location' and 'shape'.

definition_short: The smallest convex shape that contains all the points in a set.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a wooden board with nails sticking out of it at random spots. If you take a large rubber band and snap it around all the nails, the shape the rubber band makes is the 'Convex Hull'. It’s the 'boundary' of the points.
real_world_example: Collision detection in games: it's easier to check if a car hits a simple 'hull' shape than checking every tiny detail of the car's body.
technical_definition: The smallest convex set that contains a given set of points S in a Euclidean space.
common_misconceptions: It must be a circle. (False: It is usually a polygon (straight edges) that connects the outermost points).

comparisons.note: A Bounding Box is always a simple rectangle; a Convex Hull is a tighter, more custom shape.

definition_short: A fast and popular algorithm for finding the Convex Hull in O(N log N) time.
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. Pick the lowest point. 2. Sort all other points based on their 'angle' from that point. 3. Walk through the sorted points and keep only the ones that make a 'left turn'. If you have to make a 'right turn', you remove the last point because it's 'inside' the hull.
real_world_example: A robot vacuum tracing the outer walls of a room based on the 'points' where it bumped into things.
technical_definition: An algorithm for computing the convex hull of a finite set of points in the plane using a stack to maintain the hull and processing points by angle.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Graham Scan step-by-step: sorting by angle and checking turns]
common_misconceptions: It's the simplest algorithm. (False: Jarvis March is simpler to understand, but Graham Scan is much faster for large numbers of points).

comparisons.note: Graham Scan is O(N log N); Jarvis March is O(N*H) where H is hull size.

definition_short: A simple Convex Hull algorithm also known as Gift Wrapping.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Start at the leftmost point. Now, imagine wrapping a long string around the whole set of points. You 'wrap' to the point that is most 'to the left' from your current spot. You keep going until you come back to the start. It’s like wrapping a present.
real_world_example: Physically walking around a group of people to find the ones on the outer edge.
technical_definition: An algorithm for computing the convex hull with O(N*H) complexity, where H is the number of vertices on the hull.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing the Jarvis March 'Gift Wrapping' process starting from a point]
common_misconceptions: It is always slow. (False: If almost all points are inside and only a few make the 'hull', Jarvis March can actually be very fast).

comparisons.note: Jarvis March is simpler to code but usually slower than Graham Scan.

definition_short: Finding the two points in a large set that are nearest to each other.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have 1,000,000 dots on a screen. Which two are closest? Instead of checking every pair (which would take trillions of checks), this algorithm splits the screen in half (Divide and Conquer) to find the answer much faster.
real_world_example: Air traffic control: finding which two planes are closest to each other to prevent a collision.
