technical_definition: A problem in computational geometry solved in O(N log N) time by recursively finding the closest pair in halves and checking a narrow 'strip' in the middle.
common_misconceptions: You have to check every pair. (False: The O(N log N) algorithm proves you can skip most pairs by organizing the points first).

comparisons.note: Brute force is O(N²); the efficient algorithm is O(N log N).

definition_short: Calculating the exact point where two lines cross, or determining if they cross at all.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Crash Point'. If two cars are driving in straight lines, where will they hit? The algorithm uses math (usually coordinates and slopes) to find that one X, Y spot where both lines exist.
real_world_example: A graphics program deciding if a line you drew crosses over a circle or another line.
technical_definition: A procedure to find the intersection of two line segments, often using the cross product of vectors to determine if they even overlap.
common_misconceptions: Lines always cross. (False: Parallel lines never cross, and 'segments' might be too short to reach each other).

comparisons.note: Line intersection checks 2 lines; Sweep Line checks *all* intersections in a large group.

definition_short: Determining if a specific point is inside or outside a complex shape.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a map of a city (the polygon). You are standing at a specific GPS spot (the point). Are you 'Inside' the city limits or 'Outside'? One easy trick is drawing a line from your spot to the edge; if you cross the wall an odd number of times, you were inside!
real_world_example: Geofencing: Your phone sending an alert when you enter a specific shop's 'Digital Boundary'.
technical_definition: A geometric problem typically solved using the Ray Casting algorithm (counting intersections with edges) or the Winding Number algorithm.
common_misconceptions: It only works for simple boxes. (False: It works for any shape, even weird star shapes or shapes with holes).

comparisons.note: Convex Hull creates the shape; Point in Polygon checks if you are in that shape.

definition_short: Dividing a plane into regions based on which 'center point' is closest.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have 10 fire stations in a city. You draw boundaries so that every house is in the 'zone' of the fire station closest to it. The map ends up looking like a mosaic of cells. Each cell is a Voronoi region.
real_world_example: A delivery app showing you which restaurant is 'your' local branch based on distance.
technical_definition: A partitioning of a plane into regions close to each of a given set of objects (seeds), where each region consists of points closer to that seed than any other.
common_misconceptions: The cells are always regular squares. (False: They are custom polygons with different shapes based on the density of the points).

comparisons.note: They are mirrors: the center of a Delaunay circle is a Voronoi corner.

definition_short: A way to connect points to form triangles such that no point is inside the circle of any triangle.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Best Triangle Map'. If you want to connect points to make a 3D model, you don't want 'skinny, long' triangles. Delaunay math ensures the triangles are as 'fat' and balanced as possible. It is the geometric opposite of a Voronoi Diagram.
real_world_example: Creating the terrain in a video game (mountains, valleys) by connecting height-points with triangles.
technical_definition: A triangulation DT(P) such that no point in P is inside the circumcircle of any triangle in DT(P), maximizing the minimum angle.
common_misconceptions: It is just any triangles. (False: It follows strict rules to prevent 'bad' geometry used in 3D rendering).

comparisons.note: If you connect the centers of Voronoi cells, you get the Delaunay Triangulation.

definition_short: A technique that solves geometric problems by 'sliding' an imaginary line across the plane.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a vertical ruler sliding from the left side of your screen to the right. As it hits points or lines, it pauses and does a quick calculation. This lets you solve complex problems (like 'Find all crossings') without looking at every part of the screen at once.
real_world_example: A scanner in a factory detecting every object on a conveyor belt as they pass through a beam of light.
technical_definition: An algorithmic paradigm that uses a conceptual line swept through the plane to solve problems by processing only the 'events' where the line hits an object.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a vertical 'sweep line' moving across points and segments]
common_misconceptions: The line moves continuously. (False: The algorithm 'jumps' from one object to the next; it only stops at important 'Event Points').

comparisons.note: Brute force checks everything against everything; Sweep Line only checks things that are currently 'near' the line.

definition_short: An improved Max Flow algorithm that sends flow through 'large' capacity pipes first.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a water system with huge mains and tiny pipes. Normal algorithms might waste time trying to send water through the tiny pipes first. Capacity scaling says: 'Only use the big pipes first!'. Once the big pipes are full, then you start using the medium ones, then the small ones.
real_world_example: A delivery network prioritizing the use of cargo planes and semi-trucks before using smaller delivery vans.
technical_definition: A heuristic for the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm that maintains a threshold Δ and only considers edges with residual capacity ≥ Δ, reducing the number of augmentations.
syntax_or_example: while(delta > 0) { find_path_with_capacity(delta); delta /= 2; }
common_misconceptions: It finds a different flow. (False: It finds the same Max Flow, just much faster for certain types of networks).

comparisons.note: Capacity Scaling is an optimization that makes Ford-Fulkerson faster by picking 'high-value' paths first.

definition_short: A highly efficient algorithm for calculating Max Flow using level graphs and blocking flows.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is one of the 'Heavyweight' algorithms. Instead of finding one path at a time, Dinic's divides the graph into layers. It sends as much water as possible through all the pipes in one layer at the same time. It's incredibly fast for complex networks.
real_world_example: Managing traffic flow in a huge city by directing whole blocks of cars simultaneously through multiple lanes.
technical_definition: An O(V² E) algorithm for finding max flow by repeatedly building a level graph with BFS and finding blocking flows with DFS.
common_misconceptions: It is always the best choice. (False: For small graphs, simple Ford-Fulkerson is easier to code and plenty fast).

comparisons.note: Dinic's is faster than Edmonds-Karp for large, dense graphs.

definition_short: The most efficient way to assign N jobs to N workers to minimize total cost.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have 5 tasks and 5 workers. Each worker charges a different price for each task. You want to assign one task to each person so the *total* bill is as low as possible. This algorithm finds that perfect combination.
real_world_example: Assigning students to dorm rooms based on their preferences so that the most students are happy.
technical_definition: A combinatorial optimization algorithm that solves the assignment problem in O(N³) time.
common_misconceptions: It only finds the minimum. (False: You can use it to find the maximum by simply subtracting the costs from a large number first).

comparisons.note: Hungarian Algorithm focuses on *Cost*; Stable Matching focuses on *Preferences*.

definition_short: A very fast algorithm for finding the Maximum Bipartite Matching.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are a school counselor matching 100 students to 100 internships. You want the absolute maximum number of matches. Hopcroft-Karp is a super-fast way to do this for large groups, much faster than a standard search.
real_world_example: Large-scale matching systems like assigning millions of tasks to workers on a cloud platform.
technical_definition: An O(E√V) algorithm for finding a maximum cardinality matching in a bipartite graph.
syntax_or_example: // Combines BFS and DFS logic to find multiple paths at once.
common_misconceptions: It works for all graphs. (False: It specifically only works for 'Bipartite' graphs (two separate groups connecting to each other)).

comparisons.note: Bipartite matching can be solved by Max Flow, but Hopcroft-Karp is faster specifically for matching.

definition_short: Finding a pairing between two groups where no two people would rather be with each other than their current partners.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'No Regrets' matching. If you pair students and colleges, a matching is 'Stable' if no student likes a different college *and* that college also likes that student better. Everyone is at least with their best 'available' option.
real_world_example: The real system used to match medical school graduates to hospitals for their residency in the US.
technical_definition: A condition in the stable marriage problem where no 'blocking pair' exists (two elements who both prefer each other over their current matches).
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a stable matching between two lists of preferences]
common_misconceptions: Everyone gets their #1 choice. (False: You might get your 3rd choice, but you won't 'envy' anyone else because they wouldn't want you back).

comparisons.note: Hungarian minimizes total money; Stable Matching prevents jealousy/envy.

definition_short: The algorithm used to solve the Stable Matching problem.
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. Each 'Proposer' (e.g., Student) asks their #1 favorite 'Receiver' (e.g., College). 2. The College says 'Maybe' if they are free or if this student is better than their current 'Maybe'. 3. This continues until everyone is matched. It is proven to always find a stable result.
real_world_example: Assigning students to high schools based on their preference lists and the school's entrance requirements.
technical_definition: An O(N²) algorithm that provides a solution to the stable marriage problem by iteratively processing proposals from one group to the other.
syntax_or_example: while(man m is free) { w = m's next favorite; m proposes to w; }
common_misconceptions: It is fair for both sides. (False: The group that does the 'proposing' usually gets much better results than the group that has to wait to be picked).

comparisons.note: Stable Matching is the 'Problem'; Gale-Shapley is the 'Algorithm'.

definition_short: A flow problem where edges have a *minimum* required flow, not just a maximum limit.
explanation: Simple Explanation: In a standard pipe system, you can send 0 to 10 liters. With 'Demands', some pipes *must* carry at least 3 liters or the system fails. It’s used when you have a minimum quota to meet.
real_world_example: An electricity grid where every city has a minimum amount of power it *needs* to stay running.
technical_definition: A variation of the max flow problem where each edge has a lower bound l(e) and an upper bound c(e), requiring l(e) ≤ f(e) ≤ c(e).
syntax_or_example: [Image showing flow edges with [min, max] capacity labels]
common_misconceptions: It always has a solution. (False: If your pipes are too small to meet the minimum demands, no valid flow exists).

comparisons.note: Standard Max Flow only has upper limits (max); this adds lower limits (min).

definition_short: A randomized algorithm that always gives the correct answer but might take longer than expected.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Perfectionist' gambler. It uses random numbers to work, but it refuses to stop until it has the perfect correct answer. The only 'luck' involved is how long it takes to finish.
real_world_example: Randomized Quick Sort: it picks a random pivot every time. It *always* sorts correctly, but on some lucky days, it finishes faster than others.
technical_definition: A randomized algorithm whose time complexity is a random variable, but which always produces a correct result or informs about a failure.
syntax_or_example: while(answer_not_found) { try_random_path(); }
common_misconceptions: It might be wrong. (False: That is a 'Monte Carlo' algorithm. Las Vegas is always right).

comparisons.note: Las Vegas is always correct (time varies); Monte Carlo always finishes (accuracy varies).

definition_short: A randomized algorithm that always finishes fast but has a small chance of being wrong.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Fast but Risky' gambler. It guarantees it will give you an answer in 5 seconds. Usually, that answer is right. But there's a 1% chance it could be wrong. You use this when being 'mostly' right is okay.
real_world_example: The Miller-Rabin primality test: it checks if a number is prime very fast, but there is a tiny chance it says 'Yes' for a composite number.
technical_definition: A randomized algorithm whose running time is deterministic, but whose output may be incorrect with a certain probability.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a target area with random dots used to estimate size (Monte Carlo method)]
common_misconceptions: It's just guessing. (False: It uses mathematical probability to make the chance of error extremely small, like one in a trillion).

comparisons.note: Monte Carlo is fast but risky; Las Vegas is slow but perfect.

definition_short: Randomly picking 'k' items from a giant list without knowing the total size ahead of time.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a bottomless bucket of balls being poured past you. You want to pick 5 random balls. You don't know when the pouring will stop. This algorithm lets you keep a perfectly random 'sample' even if 1 billion balls go by.
real_world_example: A social media site picking a random sample of 1000 'Live' comments to show on a screen during a massive stream.
technical_definition: A family of randomized algorithms for choosing a simple random sample without replacement of k items from a population of unknown size n in a single pass.
syntax_or_example: if(rand() % current_size < k) replace_one_in_reservoir();
common_misconceptions: You just pick the first ones. (False: That wouldn't be fair to the balls that arrive later. This math ensures every ball in the infinite stream has the exact same chance of being picked).

comparisons.note: Simple sampling needs to know the total 'N' first; Reservoir works for unknown/infinite 'N'.

definition_short: The mathematical logic used to build and search a Skip List efficiently.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It uses 'Coin Flips' to build a fast search. When you add a new number, you flip a coin. If it's Heads, you add that number to the 'Express' level. If it's Heads again, you add it to the 'Super Express' level. This random building ensures the list stays balanced for fast searches.
real_world_example: Choosing which cities get an 'Airport' versus just a 'Bus Station' based on random growth to ensure a travel network stays fast.
technical_definition: The use of random level assignment for nodes to achieve O(log n) expected search, insertion, and deletion time in a linked list structure.
syntax_or_example: while(flipCoin() == HEADS) level++; newNode.levels = level;
common_misconceptions: It is always O(log n). (False: On a 'very unlucky' day where you never flip heads, it could become slow like a normal list; but this is mathematically impossible for large lists).

comparisons.note: AVL uses strict math to balance; Skip List uses coin flips/luck to balance.

definition_short: The mathematical steps to check set membership with zero memory using multiple hashes.
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. Take your word. 2. Calculate 3 different numbers for it (hashes). 3. Mark those 3 spots in a bit array. To check if a word is there later, just see if those same 3 spots are marked. If even one is empty, the word is definitely not there!
real_world_example: A spellchecker quickly checking if a word is NOT in the dictionary before wasting time looking through a massive file.
technical_definition: A procedure involving multiple independent hash functions mapping an element to bits in a bit vector to perform set membership queries with no false negatives.
common_misconceptions: It stores the word. (False: It only stores 'Marks' (1s and 0s); you can never get the original word back from the filter).

comparisons.note: Hash Table is exact; Bloom Filter is a 'maybe' that saves 99% of memory.

definition_short: A technique for quickly estimating how similar two large sets are.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have two massive books and want to know how many words they share. Instead of comparing every word, you 'shuffle' the words and just look at the first one that appears in both. By doing this many times, you get a very accurate 'fingerprint' of their similarity.
real_world_example: Search engines like Google use MinHash to detect duplicate or near-duplicate web pages across billions of sites.
technical_definition: An algorithm for estimating the Jaccard similarity coefficient between two sets by using a family of random hash functions and comparing the minimum hash values.
syntax_or_example: J(A,B) ≈ P(min(h(A)) = min(h(B)))
common_misconceptions: It gives an exact similarity score. (False: It is a probabilistic estimation that gets more accurate as you use more hash functions).

comparisons.note: Jaccard is the 'True' math; MinHash is the fast 'Estimation' for big data.

definition_short: An algorithm used to count unique elements in a massive stream of data using very little memory.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you want to count how many unique users visited your site, you could save every ID, but that uses too much RAM. HyperLogLog looks at the binary 'zeros' in the hash of an ID. If it sees a long row of zeros, it knows there are likely millions of unique users. It's like guessing the number of people in a crowd by looking at how many people have rare last names.
real_world_example: Reddit or Facebook counting 'Unique Views' on a post without storing every single user ID in a list.
technical_definition: A probabilistic data structure for the cardinality estimation problem, which can estimate the number of distinct elements in a multiset with a standard error of 2% using only 1.5 KB of memory.
common_misconceptions: It can tell you if a specific user visited. (False: It only tells you the 'Total Count' of unique users; it doesn't store the actual IDs).

comparisons.note: Bloom Filter checks 'Is this item here?'; HyperLogLog asks 'How many unique items are here?'.

definition_short: A frequency table used to count how many times items appear in a giant data stream.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s like a 'Summary Table'. Instead of a massive list, it uses a small grid and several hash functions. When an item arrives, it 'votes' in a few spots in the grid. To find the count later, you look at those spots and pick the smallest number. It might slightly overestimate, but it never underestimates.
real_world_example: A router tracking which IP addresses are sending the most traffic (Heavy Hitters) during a cyber attack.
technical_definition: A sub-linear space probabilistic data structure that serves as a frequency map of elements in a stream, providing estimates of individual item frequencies.
common_misconceptions: It is perfectly accurate. (False: It is a 'sketch', meaning there is a small chance of error (over-estimation) due to hash collisions).

comparisons.note: Hash Tables are 100% accurate but grow huge; Count-Min Sketch is small and fixed-size but slightly approximate.

definition_short: Using a simple 'best right now' rule to find a 'good enough' answer to an impossible math problem.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Some problems are so hard that a computer would take 100 years to find the perfect answer. Greedy Approximation says: 'Let's just take the best step possible every time'. It won't be perfect, but it will be very close and finished in seconds.
real_world_example: Packing a suitcase: you don't check every combination; you just put the biggest thing in first, then the next biggest, and so on.
technical_definition: An algorithm that makes a sequence of locally optimal choices to produce a solution that is within a provable factor (approximation ratio) of the global optimum.
syntax_or_example: // Choice = Pick max(value/weight); // Standard Greedy Approx for Knapsack.
common_misconceptions: It always finds the best answer. (False: It finds a 'sub-optimal' answer that is guaranteed to be 'close' to the best).

comparisons.note: Exact finds the 100% best answer (slow); Approximation finds a 95-99% good answer (fast).

definition_short: A fast way to find a small set of nodes that touches every edge in a graph.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To guard every hallway (edge) in a building, you need to pick rooms (vertices) to place guards. Finding the *minimum* number of guards is very hard. This approximation trick says: 'Pick any hallway, put guards in BOTH rooms at its ends, and delete all connected hallways'. It uses at most twice as many guards as needed.
real_world_example: Placing security cameras at intersections so that every street is covered by at least one camera.
technical_definition: A 2-approximation algorithm for the NP-Hard Vertex Cover problem that picks an edge (u, v), adds both endpoints to the cover, and removes all incident edges.
common_misconceptions: Picking the highest degree node is better. (Surprisingly, the simple 'pair-picking' method has a better mathematical guarantee than the 'pick the popular node' method).

comparisons.note: Exact is O(2^n) (Impossible for large graphs); Approx is O(V+E) (Instant).

definition_short: A fast method to find a short route through many cities for the Traveling Salesperson Problem.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To visit 100 cities, a computer can't check every route. A simple 'TSP Approx' is: 1. Build a 'Minimum Spanning Tree' (connect all cities cheaply). 2. Walk around that tree. This path is guaranteed to be no more than twice as long as the perfect route.
real_world_example: A delivery van planning its route for the day without spending 5 hours calculating the perfect path.
technical_definition: Algorithms like Christofides' or the MST-heuristic that find Hamiltonian cycles within a constant factor of the optimal length.
common_misconceptions: Nearest Neighbor is the best approx. (False: The 'Nearest Neighbor' rule actually has no upper bound on how bad it can be; MST-based methods are safer).

comparisons.note: MST heuristic is a 2-approx; Christofides is a better 1.5-approx.

definition_short: Fitting items of different sizes into the smallest number of bins.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have many different sized files and you want to fit them into as few 4GB USB drives as possible. Finding the perfect fit is hard. A 'First Fit' approach says: 'Just put the file in the first drive that has enough space'. It's quick and usually very efficient.
real_world_example: Loading boxes into shipping containers or scheduling tasks onto computer servers.
technical_definition: A combinatorial NP-hard problem in which objects of different volumes must be packed into a finite number of bins of a fixed capacity in a way that minimizes the number of bins used.
syntax_or_example: Algorithm: First Fit Decreasing (FFD) // Sort items largest to smallest first.
common_misconceptions: Filling one bin at a time is best. (False: The 'First Fit' rule, where you check all open bins, is mathematically much better than just focusing on one).

comparisons.note: Knapsack has 1 bin and you want max value; Bin Packing has infinite bins and you want to use the minimum count.

definition_short: Picking the fewest 'groups' to cover all individual items.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have a list of skills you need for a project and a list of people, each with different skill sets. You want to hire the fewest people to cover all skills. The greedy rule is: 'Always hire the person who covers the most *remaining* skills'.
real_world_example: A radio station picking the fewest number of towers to cover an entire state with signal.
technical_definition: An algorithm for the Set Cover problem that achieves an O(log n) approximation ratio by repeatedly choosing the set that contains the largest number of uncovered elements.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing sets overlapping to cover a universe of points]
common_misconceptions: It always finds the minimum people. (False: It can sometimes be slightly off, but it's guaranteed to be within O(log N) of the best).

comparisons.note: Vertex Cover is a special type of Set Cover where every 'set' (vertex) covers exactly 2 'items' (endpoints of an edge).

definition_short: A fast way to get a near-perfect value for the 0/1 Knapsack problem.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Since 0/1 Knapsack is slow for large bags, we use a trick. We slightly round the 'values' of the items (like changing $99 to $100). This makes the table much smaller and the computer much faster, while giving an answer that is 99% accurate.
real_world_example: A shipping company deciding which cargo to take—rounding cents to dollars to speed up the decision-making math.
technical_definition: A Fully Polynomial Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS) that scales values to reduce the dynamic programming state space, yielding a solution within (1-ε) of the optimum.
syntax_or_example: Value' = floor(Value / scale_factor);
common_misconceptions: It's the same as Fractional Knapsack. (False: Fractional is a different problem where you can cut items; Knapsack Approx still deals with whole items).

comparisons.note: Exact is O(W*N); Approx is O(N³ / ε).

definition_short: Finding a solution by starting with a random guess and making small improvements.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are blindfolded on a mountain and want to find the top. You just take a step in whatever direction goes 'Up'. You keep doing this until you can't go any higher. It’s simple, but you might get stuck on a small hill instead of the big mountain.
real_world_example: Rearranging the furniture in your room by moving one piece at a time until it 'feels' better.
technical_definition: A heuristic method for solving computationally hard optimization problems by moving from one solution to a neighboring solution in the search space.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a search path moving from a low state to a nearby higher state]
common_misconceptions: It always finds the best solution. (False: It often gets stuck in 'Local Optima'—small peaks that aren't the highest point).

comparisons.note: Local Search only looks nearby; Global Search (like Brute Force) looks everywhere.

definition_short: An advanced local search that sometimes allows 'bad' moves to avoid getting stuck.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine the mountain climber from before. Sometimes, they take a step *downhill* on purpose. Why? Because it might help them get off a small hill so they can find the giant mountain nearby. As time goes on, they stop taking bad moves and settle at the top.
real_world_example: Hardening metal (Annealing): you heat it up (lots of random movement) and then cool it slowly so the atoms find the perfect strong structure.
technical_definition: A probabilistic technique for approximating the global optimum of a given function by mimicking the cooling process of metallurgy.
syntax_or_example: if (ΔEnergy < 0 || rand() < exp(-ΔE / Temperature)) accept_move();
common_misconceptions: It is random guessing. (False: It is very structured; it becomes less random and more 'Greedy' as the 'Temperature' drops).

comparisons.note: Hill Climbing never goes down; Annealing sometimes goes down to find a better peak.

definition_short: Solving problems by mimicking 'Survival of the Fittest'.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You start with 100 random 'guesses'. You pick the best ones (the parents), combine their parts (Crossover), and add a little randomness (Mutation) to create a new generation. After 1000 generations, you'll have an incredibly good solution.
real_world_example: NASA designing the most aerodynamic satellite antenna by 'evolving' different shapes over thousands of digital generations.
technical_definition: A search heuristic that inspired by Charles Darwin’s theory of natural evolution, using techniques such as inheritance, mutation, selection, and crossover.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Crossover and Mutation between two parent bit-strings]
common_misconceptions: It's only for AI. (False: It's used for logistics, circuit design, and any problem with too many variables to calculate manually).

comparisons.note: Annealing works with 1 solution at a time; Genetic works with a whole 'Population' of solutions.

definition_short: A search algorithm that explores only the most promising 'top-k' paths to save memory.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are in a dark forest with a flashlight. Instead of checking every path (BFS), you only look at the 3 paths that look the brightest. You ignore everything else. It’s fast and uses very little memory, but it might miss the best path hidden in the dark.
real_world_example: Google Translate: it doesn't check every possible sentence; it only keeps the top 5 most likely translations at each step.
technical_definition: A heuristic search algorithm that is an optimization of best-first search that reduces its memory requirements by keeping only a fixed number of best partial solutions.
syntax_or_example: Width = 3; // Only keep the 3 best paths at each level.
common_misconceptions: It is the same as BFS. (False: BFS remembers *every* path; Beam Search 'forgets' the bad ones to stay fast).

comparisons.note: BFS is guaranteed perfect but slow; Beam Search is fast but might miss the best answer.

definition_short: A basic local search algorithm that only moves in the direction of improvement.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the simplest way to optimize. You look at your current spot, check your neighbors, and move to the one that is better than you. You stop as soon as all neighbors are worse. It's like walking uphill in the fog until you reach a peak.
real_world_example: Adjusting the focus on a camera: you turn it slightly left or right until the image is at its sharpest.
technical_definition: An iterative algorithm that starts with an arbitrary solution and attempts to find a better one by changing a single element of the solution.
common_misconceptions: It is the best way to find a maximum. (False: It is very prone to getting stuck on 'local' peaks that aren't the actual highest point).

comparisons.note: Hill Climbing is 'Short-sighted'; Annealing is 'Strategic'.

definition_short: The most famous algorithm for solving Linear Programming (LP) problems.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a set of rules (e.g., 'You have 10 hours and $100') and you want to make the most profit. Simplex looks at the 'corners' of all possible solutions and jumps from one corner to a better one until it finds the absolute best one.
real_world_example: A factory deciding exactly how many of 'Product A' and 'Product B' to make to maximize profit with limited materials.
technical_definition: A popular algorithm for numerical solution of the linear programming problem, which works by moving along the edges of a high-dimensional polyhedron to find the optimal vertex.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a polyhedron and the Simplex algorithm jumping between vertices]
common_misconceptions: It is the only LP solver. (False: There are other 'Interior Point' methods, but Simplex is the most widely used in history).

comparisons.note: Simplex is mathematically guaranteed to find the perfect answer for linear problems; Greedy is not.

definition_short: Finding a stable matching between two sets based on their preferences.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the logic used to match two groups (like students and colleges). A match is 'Stable' if no student would rather be at a different college that *also* wants that student. It ensures there is no 'jealousy' in the final pairings.
real_world_example: Matching medical students to residency hospitals or organ donors to patients.
technical_definition: Also known as the Gale-Shapley algorithm, it solves the matching problem in O(N²) time and is guaranteed to find a stable solution for everyone.
syntax_or_example: Gale-Shapley(Men_Prefs, Women_Prefs);
common_misconceptions: It is fair for both sides. (False: The side that 'proposes' usually gets their best possible match, while the other side gets their worst 'stable' match).

comparisons.note: Hungarian optimizes 'Cost'; Stable Marriage optimizes 'Preferences'.

definition_short: The perfect algorithm for shuffling a list so that every possible order is equally likely.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To shuffle a deck of cards perfectly, you pick a random card from the pile and move it to a new 'finished' pile. Then you pick a random one from the remaining cards and move it. You repeat this until the old pile is empty. This ensures no bias.
real_world_example: A music player shuffling a playlist so you don't hear the same artist twice in a row.
technical_definition: An O(n) algorithm for generating a random permutation of a finite sequence by iterating through the list and swapping each element with a randomly chosen one from the remaining items.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing an array where elements are swapped with random trailing indices for a shuffle]\nfor(i=n-1; i>0; i--) swap(arr[i], arr[rand() % (i+1)]);
common_misconceptions: Just using `rand() % n` on every item is a good shuffle. (False: That often results in a biased shuffle where some patterns appear more than others).

comparisons.note: Sorting with random values is O(n log n); Fisher-Yates is faster at O(n).

definition_short: A way to visit every node in a tree without using recursion or an extra stack.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Normally, to visit a tree, you need a 'memory' (stack) to remember where to go back to. Morris Traversal is clever—it creates temporary 'threads' or bridges in the tree by pointing empty leaf nodes back to their parents. Once it’s done, it removes the bridges. It uses zero extra memory!
real_world_example: Exploring a forest by tying strings to trees to find your way back, and then untying them as you leave.
technical_definition: An in-order tree traversal algorithm that uses O(1) space by modifying the tree structure temporarily (creating threads) and restoring it during the traversal.
common_misconceptions: It is faster than standard traversal. (False: It is usually slightly slower because it has to modify and restore the tree, but it uses much less RAM).

comparisons.note: Standard In-order uses O(h) memory; Morris uses O(1).

definition_short: Finding the 'Majority' element in a list (the one that appears more than 50% of the time).
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a room of people with different colored shirts. If two people with different shirts leave the room, the 'Majority' color won't change. You keep 'canceling out' pairs of different items until only one remains. If a majority exists, that person will be the winner.
real_world_example: An election system finding a candidate who received more than half the total votes in a single pass.
technical_definition: An O(n) time and O(1) space algorithm that finds a majority element using a counter that increments for matches and decrements for mismatches.
syntax_or_example: if(count == 0) candidate = x; if(x == candidate) count++; else count--;
common_misconceptions: It always finds the most common item. (False: It *only* works if one item appears more than 50% of the time; for a simple 'mode', it won't work).

comparisons.note: Hash map takes O(n) space; Boyer-Moore Voting takes almost zero space (O(1)).

definition_short: An efficient algorithm to find all the critical 'Bridges' in a graph in one pass.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It uses a 'Deep Search' (DFS) to label nodes with discovery times. If it finds a path that *cannot* get back to an earlier node without using the current road, it knows that road is a Bridge. If it breaks, the graph splits.
real_world_example: Finding every single critical road in a city that, if closed for repairs, would leave some neighborhoods completely stranded.
technical_definition: An O(V+E) algorithm using DFS and 'low-link' values to identify edges that are not part of any cycle.
syntax_or_example: if (low[v] > disc[u]) edge(u,v) is a bridge;
common_misconceptions: Every edge in a tree is a bridge. (True, because trees have no cycles; Tarjan's is mainly for general graphs with many loops).

comparisons.note: tarjan_bridge finds critical edges; tarjan_scc finds circular groups in directed graphs.

definition_short: Finding an Eulerian Circuit (a path that uses every street once) in O(E) time.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You start driving randomly. When you get stuck and end up back where you started, you've found a loop. Now, you look for a city on that loop that still has 'unvisited' roads. You do another loop from there and 'stitch' it into your first loop. Repeat until every road is used.
real_world_example: Planning a route for a street sweeper to clean every single road in a neighborhood without repeating any.
technical_definition: An efficient algorithm that finds an Eulerian circuit by repeatedly finding and merging simple cycles in a graph.
common_misconceptions: It works on any graph. (False: It only works if every city has an even number of roads; otherwise, an Eulerian circuit is impossible).

comparisons.note: Both find Euler tours, but Hierholzer’s is much faster (O(E)) than Fleury's (O(E²)).

definition_short: A specific greedy strategy for graph coloring.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To color a map with the fewest colors, you should start with the cities that have the most neighbors (the 'Hardest' cities). You color those first, and then move to the easier ones. This usually helps you use fewer total colors than just going in a random order.
real_world_example: Assigning frequencies to mobile towers: start with the towers that have the most nearby neighbors to avoid interference easily.
technical_definition: A coloring algorithm that sorts vertices in descending order of their degree and applies a greedy coloring approach to minimize the chromatic number.
syntax_or_example: 1. Sort by degree; 2. Assign lowest available color.
common_misconceptions: It is perfectly optimal. (False: It is a 'Heuristic', meaning it is very good but can still be beaten by a perfect search on complex graphs).

comparisons.note: Welsh-Powell is just Greedy Coloring with a 'Smart Sort' at the beginning.

definition_short: An algorithm for finding all 'Cliques' (groups where everyone knows everyone) in a graph.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This algorithm is the 'Ultimate Friend Group' finder. It uses recursion to explore every possible combination of people and picks out only those groups where every single person is direct friends with everyone else. It's very thorough but can be slow for massive networks.
real_world_example: Analyzing a social network to find tight-knit communities or finding 'clusters' of related proteins in biological data.
technical_definition: A backtracking algorithm for finding all maximal cliques in an undirected graph.
syntax_or_example: // BK(R, P, X) where R=current clique, P=candidates, X=excluded.
common_misconceptions: It's fast for all graphs. (False: Because finding cliques is O(2^n) hard, this algorithm can become very slow on large, dense graphs).

comparisons.note: Bron-Kerbosch finds *every* Clique; Approx algorithms just find one big one.

definition_short: An 'Optimal' algorithm for finding the Convex Hull in 2D or 3D space.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s a hybrid. It splits points into small groups, finds their hulls using Graham Scan, and then wraps them all together using Jarvis March. It automatically adjusts its speed to be the fastest possible, no matter how many points are on the outer edge.
real_world_example: A graphics engine calculating the boundary of millions of particles in a physics simulation instantly.
technical_definition: An optimal O(N log H) algorithm for computing the convex hull of N points in 2D or 3D, where H is the number of points in the output hull.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing small hulls being merged into one grand hull (Chan's logic)]
common_misconceptions: It's easier than Graham Scan. (False: It is much more complex to code because it combines two other algorithms together).

comparisons.note: Graham is O(N log N); Chan's is O(N log H), which is faster if the hull is small.

definition_short: A divide-and-conquer algorithm for finding the Convex Hull, similar to Quick Sort.
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. Find the extreme left and right points. 2. Draw a line between them to split the world in half. 3. Find the point furthest from that line; it *must* be on the hull. 4. Use that point to create a triangle and ignore everything inside. Repeat for the outside parts. Just like Quick Sort, it discards data quickly.
real_world_example: A computer vision system tracing the outline of a hand based on sensor data points.
technical_definition: A method of computing the convex hull with an average complexity of O(N log N) by recursively partitioning points based on their distance from a line segment.
common_misconceptions: It is always the best. (False: Like Quick Sort, it can be O(N²) in the absolute worst case, though this is rare on real-world data).

comparisons.note: Quickhull is often faster in practice for random data but Graham Scan is more consistent.

definition_short: A simple and fast algorithm to find the Convex Hull by sorting points by their X-coordinate.
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. Sort all points from left to right. 2. Build the 'Top half' of the rubber band by walking left-to-right. 3. Build the 'Bottom half' by walking right-to-left. 4. Join them together. It’s very easy to code and very reliable.
real_world_example: Drawing a fence around a set of posts by starting at one side and moving to the other.
technical_definition: Also called Andrew's algorithm; an O(N log N) method that computes the upper and lower hulls separately and concatenates them.
syntax_or_example: if(cross_product(OA, OB) <= 0) // Check for right turn.
common_misconceptions: It is the same as Graham Scan. (False: Graham Scan sorts by *angle*; Monotone Chain sorts by *X-coordinate*, which is much easier and faster for computers to do).

comparisons.note: Monotone Chain is generally preferred in modern coding because sorting by X is simpler than sorting by Angle.

definition_short: An algorithm for 'Clipping' a shape against another shape (usually a box).
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a drawing of a star, but you want to put it inside a square frame. Any part of the star that goes outside the square must be cut off. This algorithm walks around the star and 'trims' the edges against the four walls of the square one by one.
real_world_example: A graphics engine cutting off parts of a character that are 'off-screen' so it doesn't waste time drawing them.
technical_definition: A standard polygon clipping algorithm that works by processing the polygon edges against each clip boundary in turn.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a polygon being clipped against a rectangular window step-by-step]
common_misconceptions: It works for all shapes. (True, but it is specifically optimized for 'Convex' clipping shapes; it can sometimes create artifacts with complex concave frames).

comparisons.note: Cohen-Sutherland is for clipping 'Lines'; Sutherland-Hodgman is for 'Polygons'.

definition_short: A very fast algorithm for clipping a single line against a rectangle.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It uses a 'Code System'. It divides the whole world into 9 regions (Inside, Top-Left, Right, etc.). It gives the ends of a line a 'region code'. If both codes are 'Inside', the line stays. If they are 'Far Top', the line is deleted. It’s much faster than doing complex math for every line.
real_world_example: A 2D game deciding if a laser beam hits the screen or is fired off into space.
technical_definition: A line-clipping algorithm that uses 4-bit region codes (outcodes) to quickly determine the visibility of line segments within a rectangular window.
syntax_or_example: // Outcodes: 0001 (Left), 0010 (Right), 0100 (Bottom), 1000 (Top).
common_misconceptions: It clips full shapes. (False: It is specifically designed for 'Line Segments'; for shapes like triangles, use Sutherland-Hodgman).

comparisons.note: Cohen-Sutherland is simple and fast for many lines; Liang-Barsky is more efficient for lines that actually need to be cut.

definition_short: An efficient line clipping algorithm using parametric equations.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Unlike the region-code method, this one uses 'Percentage' math. It calculates 'At what % of the line does it enter the box?' and 'At what % does it leave?'. It is much faster for 3D graphics and complex clipping.
real_world_example: Calculating how much of a line of sight is visible through a small window in a 3D simulation.
technical_definition: A line clipping algorithm that uses the parametric representation of a line and a set of inequalities to find the intersection with a clipping window.
syntax_or_example: // Line: P(t) = P1 + (P2 - P1) * t, where 0 <= t <= 1.
common_misconceptions: It's only for 2D. (False: Because it uses parametric math, it is very easy to upgrade to 3D clipping).

comparisons.note: Liang-Barsky is generally faster because it performs fewer line-box intersection calculations.

definition_short: A method for parsing mathematical expressions using a stack.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a train yard with a 'main track' and a 'side track' (the stack). Numbers go straight through the main track. Operators (+, *) have to wait on the side track. If a low-priority operator tries to pass a high-priority one, the high-priority one is pushed out first. This converts human math (3+4) into computer math (3 4 +).
real_world_example: How a calculator or a compiler turns your typed equation `(5 + 2) * 10` into machine instructions.
technical_definition: A stack-based algorithm for parsing mathematical expressions specified in infix notation into postfix notation (RPN) or an abstract syntax tree.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing operators being 'shunted' onto a stack based on precedence rules]
common_misconceptions: It calculates the answer. (False: It only 'rearranges' the math into a better format; you need a second step (Postfix evaluation) to get the actual number).

comparisons.note: Shunting-yard is the specific *algorithm* used to perform the Infix to Postfix conversion.

definition_short: A multi-stage algorithm used in computer vision to find the 'edges' in an image.
explanation: Simple Explanation: To find the outlines of objects, Canny does 4 things: 1. Blurs the image (removes noise). 2. Finds the 'sharpest' changes in color. 3. Thins those changes into fine lines. 4. Connects them together. It’s how robots 'see' the shapes of things.
real_world_example: A face-recognition app identifying the outline of your eyes and nose.
technical_definition: An image processing technique that uses a multi-stage process (Gaussian blur, Sobel gradient, non-maximum suppression, and hysteresis) to extract edges.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a raw photo and its 'Canny' version with only white outlines]
common_misconceptions: It just finds color changes. (False: It is much smarter—it filters out random dots and 'thins' lines so you get a clean outline instead of a blurry mess).

comparisons.note: Sobel is just one step (finding gradients); Canny is the full 'Professional' process.

definition_short: Grouping a set of points into 'K' clusters based on their similarity.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You have 1000 customers. You want to group them into 3 'types' (K=3). You pick 3 random 'center' spots. Customers join the center closest to them. Then, you move the centers to the middle of their new groups and repeat. Eventually, the centers stop moving and you have 3 perfect groups.
real_world_example: Netflix grouping users into 'Action fans', 'Comedy fans', and 'Horror fans' based on their watch history.
technical_definition: A vector quantization method that aims to partition n observations into k clusters in which each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing points being grouped into color-coded clusters around 3 moving centers]
common_misconceptions: It knows how many groups there are. (False: You must tell the algorithm 'K' (the number of groups) yourself; it won't guess for you).

comparisons.note: Classification uses 'Labels' (pre-taught); Clustering finds its own groups without being taught.

definition_short: An iterative algorithm to find hidden variables in data.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you see a pile of height measurements, but you don't know who is 'Male' and who is 'Female'. EM 1. Guesses the averages for each group (Expectation). 2. Uses those guesses to group the data better (Maximization). It repeats this 'Guess and Improve' cycle until it finds the most likely hidden pattern.
real_world_example: Separating two different voices recorded on one microphone.
technical_definition: An iterative method to find maximum likelihood estimates of parameters in statistical models, where the model depends on unobserved latent variables.
syntax_or_example: // Step 1: E-step (Assign probabilities); Step 2: M-step (Update parameters).
common_misconceptions: It is the same as K-means. (False: K-means gives 'Hard' answers (you are in Group A); EM gives 'Soft' answers (you are 80% likely to be in Group A)).

comparisons.note: K-means is 'Hard' clustering; EM is 'Probabilistic/Soft' clustering.

definition_short: An algorithm for finding the most likely sequence of 'hidden' events.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are indoors and see people coming in with 'Wet Umbrellas' or 'Sunglasses'. Based on these 'clues', what was the most likely weather pattern outside over the last 5 hours? Viterbi uses DP to find the single most probable 'story' that explains the clues you saw.
real_world_example: Speech recognition: turning messy sound 'clues' into the most likely sequence of words.
technical_definition: A dynamic programming algorithm for finding the most likely sequence of hidden states—called the Viterbi path—that results in a sequence of observed events.
common_misconceptions: It calculates all possibilities. (False: It uses DP to 'prune' impossible paths, making it much faster than checking every single combination).

comparisons.note: Viterbi is structurally very similar to finding the 'Shortest Path' through a grid of probabilities.

definition_short: A highly efficient string sorting algorithm using a 'Burst Trie'.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s the 'Fastest for Words'. It puts strings into a simple folder (the Trie). When a folder gets too full, it 'bursts' it into sub-folders. Once everything is in tiny buckets, it sorts those buckets. It is designed to be very friendly to a computer's CPU cache.
real_world_example: Sorting millions of website URLs or large sets of genetic data.
technical_definition: A string sorting algorithm that distributes strings into a trie and 'bursts' nodes into smaller containers to maintain cache efficiency, achieving near-linear time.
syntax_or_example: // Trie structure + sorting small buckets.
common_misconceptions: It is for numbers. (False: It is specifically designed for 'Strings' (text) and is much faster than Quick Sort for long words).

comparisons.note: Both sort based on letters/digits, but Burst Sort is much more efficient for strings of different lengths.

definition_short: A hybrid sorting algorithm used by C++ as the default 'sort' function.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s a 'No-Risks' sort. It starts with Quick Sort (fastest). If it notices that Quick Sort is becoming slow (getting stuck), it automatically switches to Heap Sort (reliable). For very small chunks, it uses Insertion Sort. It combines the best of three different worlds.
real_world_example: The `std::sort` function in C++ or the sorting used in the Linux kernel.
technical_definition: Short for Introspective Sort; it begins with Quicksort and switches to Heapsort when the recursion depth exceeds a limit, and uses Insertion Sort for small subarrays.
syntax_or_example: // if (depth > max_depth) heapSort(); else quickSort();
common_misconceptions: It is a completely new algorithm. (False: It is a 'manager' algorithm that chooses when to use other existing algorithms for maximum safety).

comparisons.note: Intro Sort is Quick Sort with a 'Safety Net' against worst-case performance.

definition_short: A hybrid sort used in Python and Java that is optimized for real-world data.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Real-world data isn't random; it often has small pieces already in order (e.g., [1, 2, 5, 3, 4, 8]). Timsort finds these 'runs' and merges them together using a smart version of Merge Sort. It is incredibly fast for data that is even 'slightly' organized.
real_world_example: The `list.sort()` in Python and `Arrays.sort()` in Java.
technical_definition: A stable hybrid sorting algorithm, derived from merge sort and insertion sort, designed to perform well on many kinds of real-world data.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing Timsort finding 'runs' of sorted data and merging them]
common_misconceptions: It is slower than Quick Sort. (False: For most real-world apps, Timsort is much more efficient because it exploits the patterns that already exist in the data).

comparisons.note: Timsort is like Merge Sort with 'Eyes'—it looks at the data first to see if it can save work.

definition_short: A stable, in-place sorting algorithm that uses 'blocks' to merge data.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It’s like Merge Sort, but it doesn't need extra memory! It divides the list into blocks and swaps the blocks around within the original array to perform the merge. It’s very complex to code but perfect when you have zero extra RAM.
real_world_example: Sorting a massive file on a tiny embedded device where you can't create a second copy of the file.
technical_definition: Also called Block Merge Sort; it sorts in O(n log n) time and O(1) space while remaining stable by using internal buffers and block-swapping logic.
syntax_or_example: // Complexity: O(n log n) time, O(1) auxiliary space.
common_misconceptions: All in-place sorts are unstable. (False: Block sort is one of the few that is both 'In-place' and 'Stable').

comparisons.note: Merge Sort needs O(N) extra space; Block Sort needs O(1).

definition_short: A variant of Heap Sort that is much faster for nearly-sorted data.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Standard Heap Sort is slow for sorted data because it breaks the order to build a heap. Smoothsort uses a different kind of 'Leonardo Heap' that stays 'mostly sorted'. If you give it a sorted list, it finishes almost instantly (O(n)).
real_world_example: Re-sorting a list that has only 1 or 2 new items added to the end.
technical_definition: A comparison-based sort using a Leonardo Heap; it has O(n log n) worst-case but O(n) best-case for pre-sorted inputs.
common_misconceptions: It's just Heap Sort. (False: The 'Leonardo' tree structure is much more complex and powerful than a standard binary heap).

comparisons.note: Heap Sort is always O(n log n); Smoothsort can be O(n) for sorted data.

definition_short: A sort inspired by the card game Solitaire (Patience).
explanation: Simple Explanation: 1. You deal cards into piles. A card can only go on a pile if it's smaller than the top card. If it's bigger than all piles, start a new pile on the right. 2. Once all cards are in piles, merge the piles. It’s famous because the number of piles you make is exactly the 'Longest Increasing Subsequence'.
real_world_example: A person sorting a messy deck of cards by placing them into organized stacks on a table.
technical_definition: An O(n log n) sorting algorithm that forms piles according to solitaire rules and then merges them using a priority queue.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing cards being placed into stacks for Patience Sorting]
common_misconceptions: It is only for cards. (False: It is a highly efficient way for computers to find the LIS of a dataset).

comparisons.note: Patience sorting is the fastest way to find the Longest Increasing Subsequence.

definition_short: A non-comparison sort that uses 'Distribution' to sort nearly in-place.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you know your numbers are between 1 and 100, you can guess where each number belongs. 10 belongs near the start, 90 near the end. Flashsort 'flashes' each number to its approximate home, then does a tiny bit of final cleaning to finish the job. It's incredibly fast for evenly spread data.
real_world_example: Sorting people into a stadium: if someone has seat #450, you tell them to 'Flash' straight to the middle section instead of checking every row.
technical_definition: A distribution sort that partitions elements into buckets based on their value and then uses in-place insertion sort to refine the order.
syntax_or_example: // index = (n - 1) * (A[i] - min) / (max - min);
common_misconceptions: It needs a lot of extra memory. (False: It is one of the few distribution sorts that is 'mostly in-place', requiring very little extra RAM).

comparisons.note: Flashsort is like Bucket Sort but uses much less memory by doing most work in-place.

definition_short: A faster Insertion Sort that leaves 'gaps' to avoid shifting.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a librarian leaving empty spots on a shelf. When a new book arrives, they don't have to push 50 books over; they just put it in the gap. In code, this 'Gapped Array' trick turns the slow O(n²) Insertion Sort into a much faster O(n log n) sort.
real_world_example: Leaving blank lines in your notebook so you can add more notes later without rewriting the whole page.
technical_definition: A sorting algorithm that employs an insertion sort on a larger-than-necessary array with gaps to achieve O(n log n) average time complexity.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing an array with blank 'null' spots between numbers in Library Sort]
common_misconceptions: It saves memory. (False: It intentionally uses *more* memory (empty spaces) to gain a huge speed boost).

comparisons.note: Library Sort is the 'Turbo' version of Insertion Sort for large lists.

definition_short: Least Significant Digit Radix Sort.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You sort a list of numbers by looking at the *last* digit first (ones place). Then you sort by the tens, then hundreds. By working 'right to left', the sort stays stable and perfectly handles numbers with the same digits.
real_world_example: Sorting a pile of index cards by Day, then by Month, then by Year.
technical_definition: A non-comparison sort that processes digits from right to left using a stable sub-sort (usually Counting Sort).
syntax_or_example: // Sort '123': 1. Sort by '3'; 2. Sort by '2'; 3. Sort by '1'.
common_misconceptions: It only works for base-10. (False: It can sort binary data or letters too).

comparisons.note: LSD is 'Right-to-Left' and is best for integers; MSD is 'Left-to-Right' and is best for words.

definition_short: Most Significant Digit Radix Sort.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You sort by the *first* letter/digit first. This splits the data into 'A' words, 'B' words, etc. Then you sort *inside* those groups. It's the 'Divide and Conquer' version of Radix Sort and is perfect for alphabetical sorting.
real_world_example: Sorting a dictionary: you sort all 'A' words first, then go to 'B'.
technical_definition: A radix sort that processes digits from left to right, typically implemented recursively by partitioning the input into sub-buckets.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing strings being grouped into buckets by their first character in MSD Radix Sort]
common_misconceptions: It's just as simple as LSD. (False: MSD is recursive and more complex to code than the simple loop of LSD).

comparisons.note: MSD is better for sorting strings or numbers of different lengths.

definition_short: The full mathematical procedure for parallel sorting.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It works by turning a random list into 'mountains' (increasing then decreasing). Then, it uses a 'butterfly' pattern of comparisons to smash the mountain into a flat sorted line. It is used in GPUs because it can compare thousands of numbers at once.
real_world_example: The hardware logic inside a graphics card sorting pixels for a 4K video game.
technical_definition: A sorting network algorithm with O(n log² n) complexity, based on the principle of merging bitonic sequences using parallel comparators.
common_misconceptions: It is fast on a normal PC. (False: On a single-core CPU, it is much slower than Merge Sort; it only wins when you have hundreds of parallel cores).

comparisons.note: Bitonic Sort is essentially the parallel version of Merge Sort.

definition_short: A parallel algorithm for sorting data in a 2D grid.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine sorting a group of people standing in a square grid (like a marching band). 1. Sort every row left-to-right. 2. Sort every column top-to-bottom. 3. Repeat. Eventually, the whole grid is sorted from Top-Left to Bottom-Right.
real_world_example: A massive warehouse floor with robots rearranging pallets in rows and columns simultaneously.
technical_definition: A parallel sorting algorithm for a 2D mesh of processors that alternates between row-wise and column-wise sorting to achieve O(√n log n) time.
syntax_or_example: // Round 1: Sort rows; Round 2: Sort cols; // Repeat log(N) times.
common_misconceptions: It is for simple lists. (False: It is specifically designed for 2D 'Grids' or 'Meshes' of data).

comparisons.note: Bitonic uses a 'Network' of wires; Shear Sort uses a 'Grid' of cells.

definition_short: A parallel version of Bubble Sort that compares Odd and Even pairs alternately.
explanation: Simple Explanation: In Round 1, all 'Odd-Even' pairs (indices 1&2, 3&4...) compare and swap. In Round 2, all 'Even-Odd' pairs (indices 0&1, 2&3...) compare and swap. Because different pairs can swap at the same time, this sort is very fast on parallel machines.
real_world_example: A group of dancers in two lines who only swap with their immediate partners on the 'Odd' beat or 'Even' beat.
technical_definition: Also called Brick Sort; a comparison sort that iterates through the list alternately comparing adjacent odd-indexed/even-indexed pairs.
common_misconceptions: It is faster than Quick Sort. (False: It's still based on Bubble Sort, so it's O(n²), just easier for parallel hardware to run).

comparisons.note: Bubble Sort handles one swap at a time; Odd-Even handles many swaps at once.

definition_short: A recursive sorting algorithm that is famous for being incredibly slow.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It works like this: 1. If the ends are wrong, swap them. 2. Sort the first 2/3rds. 3. Sort the last 2/3rds. 4. Sort the first 2/3rds *again*. It is a joke algorithm used to show how *not* to use recursion.
real_world_example: A worker who cleans the first half of the room, then the second half, then re-cleans the first half just to be sure.
technical_definition: A recursive sorting algorithm with a terrible time complexity of O(n^2.7), slower than even standard Bubble Sort.
syntax_or_example: stooge(arr, 0, 2n/3); stooge(arr, n/3, n); stooge(arr, 0, 2n/3);
common_misconceptions: It is useful. (False: It is strictly for academic purposes to analyze inefficient recursion).

comparisons.note: Quick Sort is O(N log N); Stooge Sort is O(N^2.7). Quick Sort is millions of times faster.

definition_short: The full mathematical logic of the garden gnome sort.
explanation: Simple Explanation: The 'Gnome' moves forward if things are right. If he finds a mistake, he swaps and steps *backwards* until the mistake is fixed, then continues moving forward. It’s like a person organizing a shelf by only moving one item at a time until it's perfect.
real_world_example: Sorting a handful of receipts by checking each one against the previous one and shifting back if one is out of order.
technical_definition: A sorting algorithm that sorts a list with a single loop, performing swaps and moving the index back when a swap occurs, achieving O(n²) worst-case time.
syntax_or_example: while(i < n) { if(arr[i] >= arr[i-1]) i++; else { swap(arr[i], arr[i-1]); i--; } }
common_misconceptions: It's the same as Bubble Sort. (False: It follows a different 'path' through the data, looking more like an Insertion Sort without a nested loop).

comparisons.note: Gnome sort is structurally simpler but performs more total swaps.

definition_short: The distribution logic for sorting decimals and uniform data.
explanation: Simple Explanation: You create 10 'Buckets'. You put 0.1s in Bucket 1, 0.2s in Bucket 2, etc. Then you sort each bucket. This is perfect for data that is 'evenly spread out'. If the data is random, it's almost as fast as O(n).
real_world_example: A grocery store worker sorting fruits: apples in one bin, oranges in another, then arranging the apples within their bin.
technical_definition: A distribution sort that partitions an array into several buckets, each containing a range of values, and then applies another sort to each bucket.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing data points being distributed into array-buckets based on a value range]
common_misconceptions: It is always linear. (False: If all items fall into *one* bucket, it takes the time of the underlying sort, like O(N²)).

comparisons.note: Counting Sort is for 'Whole Numbers'; Bucket Sort is for 'Decimals/Ranges'.

definition_short: The mathematical study of networks consisting of points (nodes) and lines (edges).
explanation: Simple Explanation: It is the study of how things are connected. Whether it is people on Facebook, cities on a map, or computers on the internet, everything can be drawn as a 'graph'. It helps us find the shortest path, detect groups, and solve puzzles.
real_world_example: Google Maps uses graph theory to find the fastest way for you to get to college.
technical_definition: The study of graphs, which are mathematical structures used to model pairwise relations between objects from a certain collection.
syntax_or_example: G = (V, E) where V is a set of vertices and E is a set of edges.
common_misconceptions: Graphs only mean bar charts. (False: In Computer Science, a graph is a network of nodes and connections).

comparisons.note: Set Theory deals with collections of objects; Graph Theory deals with the *relationships* between those objects.

definition_short: A graph containing a trail that visits every edge exactly once.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you are a painter who needs to paint every line in a drawing without ever lifting your brush or going over the same line twice. If you can do it and end up back where you started, the drawing is an Eulerian Graph.
real_world_example: A mail carrier trying to drive through every street in a neighborhood exactly once to save fuel.
technical_definition: A connected graph in which every vertex has an even degree, allowing for a circuit that traverses every edge exactly once.
syntax_or_example: \n// Rule: Every node degree must be even.
common_misconceptions: It must visit every node only once. (False: You can visit nodes as many times as you like; it is the *edges* that must be unique).

comparisons.note: Eulerian is about visiting every 'Street' (Edge); Hamiltonian is about visiting every 'House' (Node).

definition_short: A graph containing a path that visits every vertex exactly once.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is about visiting 'Places'. If you can find a route that stops at every single city on a map exactly once and then returns home, you have found a Hamiltonian cycle. It is much harder for computers to solve than Eulerian problems.
real_world_example: A delivery driver who has to stop at 10 specific houses exactly once and then return to the warehouse.
technical_definition: A graph that possesses a Hamiltonian circuit—a closed loop that passes through every vertex of the graph exactly once.
common_misconceptions: It is easy to find. (False: Finding a Hamiltonian path is an 'NP-Complete' problem, meaning it's one of the hardest things for a computer to do efficiently).

comparisons.note: Eulerian = Every Edge; Hamiltonian = Every Node.

definition_short: A graph that stays connected even if any single vertex is removed.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is a 'Strong' network. There are no 'Critical Cities'—if any one city is destroyed, all other cities can still talk to each other. There is always a backup path.
real_world_example: A high-reliability computer network where if one router fails, the internet doesn't go down for anyone else.
technical_definition: A connected graph such that it has no articulation points; it requires the removal of at least two vertices to become disconnected.
syntax_or_example: [Image showing biconnected components separated by articulation points]
common_misconceptions: It means two-way connections. (False: It refers to 'Redundancy'—having at least two separate ways to reach every point).

comparisons.note: Biconnected is for undirected graphs (path redundancy); Strongly Connected is for directed graphs (can go both ways).

definition_short: A critical edge whose removal would split a graph into two separate pieces.
explanation: Simple Explanation: It is a 'Single Point of Failure'. If you delete this one line, the graph breaks into two isolated islands. It is called a bridge because it's often the only link between two large groups.
real_world_example: A single physical bridge connecting an island to the mainland—if it breaks, the island is cut off.
technical_definition: An edge in an undirected connected graph is a bridge if and only if it is not contained in any cycle.
common_misconceptions: Every road is a bridge. (False: If there's another way around—a cycle—then that road is NOT a bridge).

comparisons.note: Bridge is a 'Road' (edge); Articulation Point is a 'City' (node).

definition_short: A number that measures how much a graph looks like a tree.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Trees are easy for computers to solve. General graphs are hard. Tree Width tells you 'how close' a messy graph is to being a simple tree. If a graph has a low Tree Width, we can use 'Tree tricks' to solve it very fast.
real_world_example: A complex circuit board that is mostly organized in branches but has a few messy cross-links.
technical_definition: The minimum width among all possible tree decompositions of a graph, where width is the size of the largest set in the decomposition minus one.
syntax_or_example: TreeWidth(Tree) = 1; TreeWidth(Cycle) = 2;
common_misconceptions: It is the same as the width of a tree. (False: It is a measure for *any* graph, not just trees).

comparisons.note: Diameter is the 'length' of a graph; Tree Width is the 'complexity' of its branching.

definition_short: The proof that any flat map can be colored with just 4 colors so no neighbors match.
explanation: Simple Explanation: No matter how messy you draw a map of countries, you only ever need 4 colors to make sure no two countries touching each other have the same color. It’s a famous rule that applies to all 'Planar' (flat) graphs.
real_world_example: An artist coloring a world map—they never need more than 4 colored pencils.
technical_definition: A theorem stating that the vertices of every planar graph can be colored with at most four colors such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color.
common_misconceptions: It works for 3D maps. (False: If countries can have 'tunnels' or exist on a donut shape, you might need more than 4 colors).

comparisons.note: Four Color Theorem says the Chromatic Number of any planar graph is at most 4.

definition_short: The challenge of finding the largest group of nodes where everyone is connected to everyone else.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a huge party. You want to find the largest 'Inner Circle' where every person in that group is direct friends with every other person in that group. This is a very hard problem for computers to solve as the party gets bigger.
real_world_example: Analyzing social networks to find 'echo chambers' or tight-knit communities.
technical_definition: A classic optimization problem that involves finding the clique with the maximum number of vertices in a given undirected graph.
common_misconceptions: It is the same as the 'Complete Graph'. (False: A complete graph *is* one big clique; the problem is finding a clique *inside* a messy graph).

comparisons.note: Max-Clique finds the 'most connected' group; Independent Set finds the 'least connected' group.

definition_short: Finding the largest group of nodes where NO two nodes are connected to each other.
explanation: Simple Explanation: This is the 'Anti-Social' problem. You want to pick the largest number of people to invite to a dinner such that NO two people in the room are friends with each other (to avoid talking). It’s the exact opposite of the Clique problem.
real_world_example: Placing mobile towers so they are as far apart as possible to avoid signal interference.
technical_definition: An independent set such that adding any other vertex would cause it to no longer be independent; the problem is to find the one with maximum cardinality.
common_misconceptions: Independent nodes have zero edges. (False: They can have edges to other people, just not to the other invited guests).

comparisons.note: These are 'Complements'. The independent set of a graph is the clique of its opposite graph.

definition_short: A set of nodes whose removal leaves the remaining graph with zero cycles.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a system of pipes with many confusing loops (cycles). A Feedback Vertex Set is a list of 'Junctions' you can shut down to ensure that no water can ever flow in a circle again. The remaining pipes would look like a simple tree.
real_world_example: Finding the 'Main Breakers' in an electrical circuit that, if flipped, would stop all circular currents.
technical_definition: A set of vertices in a graph such that every cycle in the graph contains at least one vertex from the set.
syntax_or_example: // Graph - FVS = Acyclic Graph (Tree).
common_misconceptions: You should remove all nodes. (False: We want the *smallest* set of nodes that breaks all loops).

comparisons.note: After removing a Feedback Vertex Set, you can perform a Topological Sort on what's left.

definition_short: A flow in a network where the amount of data/fluid entering every node equals the amount leaving.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Think of a heart and blood vessels. In a circulation, there is no 'start' or 'end'—the fluid just moves in a closed loop. Every gallon of water that enters a pipe junction must leave it immediately. Nothing builds up.
real_world_example: A city's recycling system where every piece of plastic entering a facility must be shipped out as a new product.
technical_definition: A flow function f on a graph such that for every vertex v, the net flow is zero (Flow in = Flow out).
syntax_or_example: Σ f(in) = Σ f(out) for all vertices.
common_misconceptions: It must have a source and sink. (False: A pure circulation has no start or end; it is just a balanced loop).

comparisons.note: Max Flow has a clear Start and End; Circulation is a balanced loop.

definition_short: The minimum amount of flow a node *must* receive in a network.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine a city in a power grid. A node with 'Demand' is a customer who says: 'I need at least 100 units of electricity to stay running'. If the system can't send at least 100, it fails.
real_world_example: A grocery store requiring at least 50 loaves of bread every morning to satisfy customers.
technical_definition: A value d(v) assigned to a vertex in a flow network representing the net amount of flow that must be delivered to that node.
syntax_or_example: Flow_in - Flow_out = Demand(v);
common_misconceptions: Demand is the same as Capacity. (False: Capacity is a 'Limit' (Max); Demand is a 'Requirement' (Min)).

comparisons.note: Supply is 'Giving'; Demand is 'Taking'.

definition_short: The amount of flow a node provides to the rest of the network.
explanation: Simple Explanation: In a power grid, the 'Supply' nodes are the power plants. They push energy out to everyone else. The total supply in the network must be enough to cover all the demands.
real_world_example: A warehouse that has 500 iPhones ready to be shipped out to stores.
technical_definition: A value s(v) assigned to a vertex in a flow network representing the net amount of flow produced by that node.
syntax_or_example: Flow_out - Flow_in = Supply(v);
common_misconceptions: Supply is always positive. (False: In math, we often represent Supply as positive and Demand as negative to make the total sum zero).

comparisons.note: Source is the 'Location'; Supply is the 'Amount' of data starting there.

definition_short: A rule stating that the maximum flow through a network is limited by its smallest bottleneck.
explanation: Simple Explanation: If you want to send water through a system of pipes, the most you can send is exactly equal to the capacity of the narrowest part of the system. Finding the 'Maximum Flow' is mathematically the same as finding the 'Minimum Bottleneck' (the Cut).
real_world_example: The maximum number of cars that can cross a city is limited by the narrowest bridge that everyone *must* use.
technical_definition: The maximum value of an s-t flow is equal to the minimum capacity of an s-t cut in the network.
common_misconceptions: You can have flow higher than the min-cut. (False: By definition, the min-cut is the 'narrowest' part that stops any more flow from passing).

comparisons.note: In linear math, Max Flow is the 'Primal' and Min Cut is the 'Dual' problem.

definition_short: A secondary graph showing how much more flow can be sent through each pipe.
explanation: Simple Explanation: Imagine you have a pipe that can hold 10 liters, and you are already sending 4. The 'Residual' graph shows a 6-liter capacity forward and a 4-liter capacity 'backward' (to allow you to cancel or undo the flow). It helps algorithms find new paths.
real_world_example: A hotel booking site showing 'Remaining Rooms' rather than total rooms.
technical_definition: A graph G_f used in flow algorithms that consists of edges with residual capacities (original capacity minus current flow).
syntax_or_example: [Image showing a flow edge and its corresponding residual edges with forward and backward values]
common_misconceptions: Backward edges move physical fluid. (False: They are just 'math' edges that allow the computer to change its mind and reduce flow later).

comparisons.note: You look for an Augmenting Path *inside* the Residual Network.

definition_short: A graph organized into layers based on distance from the source.
